data.title: Thames Water to escape new penalties until 2030 under rescue deal
data.description:
Thames Water is set to escape new fines until 2030 under a deal agreed between creditors and the industry watchdog to save the stricken company.

Ofwat, which regulates the sector, is planning to shield the struggling utility from financial penalties linked to poor performance in return for a commitment from creditors to invest cash in the business for the next four years.

The controversial plan would carve out Thames from a penalty scheme run by Ofwat which levies fines if water companies fall behind on key metrics such as leaks and sewer flooding.

Thames is struggling under a £20bn debt pile and will run out of money again in October without the new creditor agreement.

However, the penalties deal has angered campaigners, who say the watchdog is failing in its duty to hold water companies to account.

Feargal Sharkey, the clean water campaigner, accused Ofwat of shirking its responsibility to regulate the industry.

“Ofwat has proved yet again it is incapable, unwilling to and simply does not want to properly regulate the greed, the abuse of customers and the financial engineering that is destroying this industry and the environment,” he said.

The plan, which has been put forward by creditors including US hedge funds Elliott Management and Silver Point, first went to Ofwat for approval in June 2025.

The creditors, known as London & Valley Water, provided the utility with £3bn in emergency funding last year.

As part of the deal, some creditors like Elliott would accept a 30pc write-down in their debt while more junior creditors would lose all their money.

The creditors defended the agreement and said it would mean “clear accountability” if things went wrong.

A spokesman said: “All outstanding fines will be paid. Regulators will have enhanced transparency and Thames Water will have clear accountability for reducing pollution and improving environmental outcomes against stretching performance targets.”

Utilities such as Thames Water have to pay penalties when they fail to meet performance targets set by Ofwat for the year.

Under the new proposals, first reported by The Financial Times, these penalties would be replaced with a formal commitment to invest in Thames Water.

This would allow Ofwat to continue with investigations and penalties but any cash raised from them would be spent on fixing issues with the business rather than returned to the Treasury.

The utility could still face separate fines from other regulators like the Environment Agency and would still have to reckon with possible legal cases. All outstanding fines would also have to be paid.

The Government is reportedly keen to keep Thames Water out of temporary nationalisation but the utility has only been kept afloat so far by the £3bn emergency loan, which is expected to cost the utility an extra £800m in interest payments.

Thames Water said that if Ofwat agreed a deal, creditors had offered the company an additional £6.6bn of debt at a lower rate of interest than the emergency loan, as well as £3.35bn of equity.

A spokesman for Thames Water said: “Thames Water remains focused on securing a market-led solution which delivers improvements for customers and the environment as soon as practicable, whilst making progress with our operational and financial turnaround plan.”

An Ofwat spokesperson said: “We continue to engage with London & Valley Water, and are reviewing their plans carefully to assess whether they deliver a turnaround in the company’s operational performance and strengthen its financial resilience to the benefit of customers and the environment.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Soho House founder puts private Scottish island up for sale
data.description:
Nick Jones, the founder of Soho House, has put his private Scottish island up for sale.

Situated in the centre of Loch Lomond, Inchconnachan Island is being advertised to buyers for £3m.

However, for an extra £7m, Mr Jones will oversee the creation of a bespoke luxury retreat on the 100-acre plot.

Mr Jones has put the island up for sale six years after buying it for £1.6m with his wife, Kirsty Young, the former presenter of the BBC’s Desert Island Discs.

The island, which sits at the heart of Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park, is also known as “Wallaby Island”.

This is because of a colony of red-necked wallabies that have been living on the island since the 1940s. They were first released by Fiona Gore, the late Countess of Arran, a record-breaking speed boater.

Native to Australia, the wild marsupials survive on the island by eating oak, holly and birch. There are now around six left.

After purchasing the island in 2020, Mr Jones and Ms Young forged ahead with plans to build an eco-lodge. They were granted planning consent despite concerns by environmental campaigners that the project could disrupt the wild wallabies.

The proposed house, designed by Edinburgh-based practice Gras Architects, is a two-storey, five-bedroom lodge with a private jetty.

The plans also include a boathouse within a sheltered cove, warden’s accommodation, and mainland garage facilities. Development has not yet begun on the project.

In marketing materials advertised by Sotheby’s, Mr Jones described the sale as “a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to own an island and to care for its flora, fauna and wildlife”.

Designated an “area of special scientific interest” and a conservation area, the island is richly wooded with ancient Scots pine, oak and birch.

It is also home to the capercaillie – a large woodland grouse and one of Scotland’s most endangered birds – as well as sea eagles and nesting ospreys.

The listing comes after Mr Jones completed a take-private deal for Soho House, ending the private members’ club’s four-year run as a publicly listed company in New York.

As the chain’s chief executive, Mr Jones rolled over his 6pc stake into the new company.

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Canary Wharf is a vision of a better Britain
data.description:
I think of Canary Wharf in the same way I think of Venice. Of course, Venice’s melancholy beauty is unique. But it is a dead city. It never grows. Famously, it sinks. The aesthetics of Canary Wharf might not be universally appealing, but it is very much alive. And it is growing all the time.

Conceived in a euphoria of Thatcher-era optimism, we might well feel nostalgic for a moment when such ambition was possible. But the lessons of this area of Docklands-on-steroids, once forlorn, might still be applied to other presently neglected and decaying parts of London. Alas, there are plenty of them.

Driving south down the M11, the first sight of London is Canary Wharf in the distance. And only the stubbornly simple-minded would not find this sight thrilling. Then, getting closer to the city, especially at night, the monumental massing of the buildings is urban architecture at its most exciting. “Here,” I think to myself, “is modernity that actually works”.

Another splendid aspect of Canary Wharf is that, unlike Paris’ similar out-of-town La Défense which interrupts historic vistas on the French capital’s horizon, this new metropolis is visually stand-alone and interferes with nothing. Except maybe it interferes with complacency.

And the miracle is: this was the decrepit old East End of London whose magnificent docks had been made redundant by the revolution of containerisation. It was lugubrious and depressing. The first intimation of rebirth was a public development corporation which enabled useful infrastructure, always essential. There was City Airport and then the Docklands Light Railway (DLR). In 1987 the DLR was the most ambitious London transport initiative since the southern extension of the Northern Line in the Twenties.

But it was a developer from Kentucky, the magnificently named G. Ware Travelstead, who realised the opportunities in this Isle of Dogs desert. Where others saw hopelessness, Travelstead had a vision of prosperity. He persuaded First Boston Real Estate to take an interest. It was a private initiative that brought immense public benefit.

Not every building in Canary Wharf is an architectural masterpiece, although some are. But the great majority of the country’s tallest buildings are here. Height alone does not confer merit, but tall buildings are irrefragable evidence of urban vitality.

The landmark of Canary Wharf is César Pelli’s One Canada Square, a fantastical structure of 764ft with a distinctive pyramidal roof. It would have been more remarkable still if the architect had been allowed to make it 20 or so storeys taller, as he had intended.

One Canada Square gives a visual nod to the same architect’s Transamerica Building, a San Francisco ziggurat. And all of Canary Wharf with its dignified master plan by Skidmore, Owings and Merrill acknowledges the very best of American cities: that nocturnal drive down a six-lane highway evokes not Dundee or Peterborough, but Atlanta and Chicago. There is brio and élan and energy. Plus a suggestion, slight but persistent, that this might be the future.

To suggest that vitality, when HSBC leaves its Canary Wharf headquarters in 2027, the 42-storey building will not sit redundant, but be radically reinterpreted. It will be re-clad, façades will be opened-up, bankers will be replaced by living human beings and the roof will be green.

Cities are living things, as Canary Wharf proves. Feel the pulse! You don’t finish a city. You start it. There’s more cause for optimism in Canary Wharf than there is in Venice. It’s a rare example of confidence and commitment which should inspire every city in the country. It certainly inspires me.

data.title: Renters’ Rights Act: What the new law means for renters and landlords
data.description:
Are you a landlord affected by the Renters’ Rights Act? Sign up to the Buy-to-Let Masterclass, The Telegraph’s new five-part newsletter, for actionable advice and practical tools to make the most of your bricks and mortar.

The Renters’ Rights Act comes into force today ushering in some of the biggest changes to renting in over 30 years, according to industry body The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA).

Brought in with the aim to give tenants who rent in the private sector greater protections from eviction, unfair rent increases and greater stability of tenure, the extra bureaucracy burden on landlords has already resulted in many threatening to leave the sector altogether.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the measures laid out in the law and what they mean for landlords in England.

  • What do landlords need to do right now?
  • Renters’ Rights Act key points
  • What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?
  • New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act
  • How will it affect landlords?
  • Will it apply to existing tenancies?
  • FAQs from Telegraph readers

What do landlords need to do right now?

For landlords with an existing tenancy, there’s one task you must complete by May 31.

By this date, you must provide every tenant named on the tenancy agreement with The Renters’ Rights Act Information Sheet, either as a hard copy or as a downloadable pdf attached to an email. Emailing or texting a link to the document will not be considered valid.

This document explains how their tenancy may be affected by the new law.

If you have an agent acting on your behalf, they must provide this document – even if you have already given it to your tenants.

One exception is existing tenants without a written tenancy agreement. In this case, landlords do not need to provide the Government’s information sheet, but you must instead hand over a written statement setting out the key terms of their tenancy and other information – set out online here – due by the same date.

This is required by law. Failure to provide tenants with the required information by May 31 will result in a fine of up to £7,000.

Renters’ Rights Act key points

  • End of Section 21 “no fault” evictions: landlords wanting to regain possession of a rental property will need to serve a section 8 notice using approved grounds.
  • Rent increases can only happen once a year: any increases must reflect market rates.
  • New property standards: under the new “Decent Homes Standard”, properties must be safe, of good value and good quality.
  • End of fixed-term tenancies: new and existing tenancies must change to rolling agreements.
  • New landlord ombudsman and database: will give tenants access to impartial and binding resolution for complaints against landlords. All landlords must sign up.
  • New pet rules: tenants have the right to request pets, but landlords can stipulate pet insurance must be taken out to protect from any resulting damage.
  • Rental payment overhaul: landlords cannot accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals, and no more than one month’s rent can be paid in advance.

What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?

The Renters’ Rights Bill built on the previous Renters’ Reform Bill, introduced by the Conservatives. Now law, the Renters’ Rights Act will introduce new protections and rights for the estimated 11 million private tenants in England by reforming the current system of renting.

An end to fixed-term tenancies, longer notice periods to give renters more time to find a new home and restrictions on rent increases will give tenants more rights and landlords less control over how they manage their property and buy-to-let business.

Labour says it will “transform the experience of private renting” which will largely be achieved by ending Section 21 evictions dubbed “no-fault evictions”.

The NRLA has argued the rules could force landlords out of the sector. One in three were considering exiting the market, according to property website Rightmove, and 65pc said they felt unsupported by the Government.

New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act

Evictions and serving notice:

  • Section 21 no-fault evictions will be abolished.
  • Fixed-term tenancies will be replaced with periodic tenancies, also known as rolling contracts, that don’t have an end date.
  • Tenants will have a 12-month protected period at the start of their tenancy preventing landlords from forcing evictions to move back in or sell.
  • Tenants can serve two months’ notice to quit the property at any time.
  • Landlords must give four months’ notice if they wish to move back in or sell a rental property.
  • Landlords will not be able to evict a tenant in arrears until three months of payment have been missed, up from two months, while the notice period has been increased from two to four weeks.

Prices and payment:

  • Rent increases are limited to once a year and only to the market rate, which can be challenged by tenants at the first-tier tribunal if they believe it is excessive. Increases cannot be backdated.
  • Large amounts of rent cannot be demanded in advance of a tenancy starting.
  • Landlords must register with a digital database, failing to do so means that they cannot use certain grounds of possession.
  • Asking rents must be published to prevent bidding wars, making it illegal for landlords to accept offers higher than the rent advertised.

Standards and safety:

  • The Decent Homes Standard and Awaab’s Law, currently applicable to homes in the social housing sector, will be extended to private sector landlords to ensure homes are made safe, are of good value and good quality.
  • A private rented sector landlord ombudsman will be set up to provide an impartial and binding resolution for tenant complaints against landlords.

Discrimination:

  • Strengthened rights for tenants to have pets, although landlords can insist on pet insurance to cover any damage to their property.
  • End discrimination against tenants on benefits or with children.

Compliance:

  • Councils will have the power to issue civil penalties of up to £7,000 to landlords who don’t comply with the reforms, rising to £40,000 for a serious breach or repeat non-compliance with the law.
  • More strength will be given to rent repayment orders that can be issued by councils when landlords breach the law.

How will it affect tenants?

The Renters’ Rights Act will give tenants greater protections and stronger rights when they’re renting.

As outlined in the rules above, among the changes the new law will usher in, tenants must be given longer notice periods when their landlords want to move back in or sell the property they’re renting, the property must legally reach the Decent Homes Standard, and they will no longer be subject to frequent above-market rent increases.

For any tenants with a complaint about their landlord, the introduction of an independent ombudsman will help readdress the natural power dynamic and help tenants to reach a resolution.

How will it affect landlords?

As already noted, one of the biggest changes for landlords is the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions. Tenancies can only be ended using specific grounds under Section 8, including rental arrears and anti-social behaviour, as well as if the landlord wishes to sell or move into the property.

For the two latter reasons, an extended four-month notice period must be served.

In cases where tenants refuse to leave, you must apply to the court to evict them. This is expected to add even more pressure to an already-stretched court system.

Currently, the average time between a landlord submitting a claim for an eviction and regaining possession of their property is more than seven months.

In response to the Government’s claims that the courts will be ready for the impact of the changes, Ben Beadle, chief executive of the NRLA, said: “Seven months is an eternity for responsible landlords who may be dealing with serious rent arrears and for neighbours having to endure anti-social behaviour.”

Not only could it take longer to evict a tenant, landlords will have to endure greater levels of arrears before they apply for possession as the minimum level of arrears for eviction is bumped up from two to three months.

Banning the receipt of large sums of rent upfront, meanwhile, could put landlords off renting to international students or tenants who have had credit difficulties in the past or do not pass affordability assessments.

While the legislation has banned “rental discrimination” for those receiving benefits or who have children, it does not cover these other tenant groups.

Without the security of payment in advance, those who fall into these categories may struggle to be accepted.

Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Yes, the law applies to existing tenancies. For those with tenancies that began before May 1, landlords or agents must provide the Government’s information sheet (as explained above).

From this date, tenancy agreements can no longer have a set end date. This means all tenancy agreements will become rolling tenancies on this date – if the tenancy specified an end date, it will no longer apply.  

Landlords won’t need to re-issue tenancy agreements, it will take effect automatically.

FAQs from Telegraph readers

Ahead of the new rules, we’ve been contacted by scores of concerned Telegraph readers who are unsure about some of the law’s finer details. Here, we’ve answered some of their questions.

Assuming you are using Airbnb in the proper way, the Renters’ Rights Act will not affect the rules.

This law only applies to “assured periodic tenancies”, where the landlord does not live in the property, it is the tenants’ main accommodation and tenants have their own room (but may share other common areas such as a kitchen). These have previously been known as “assured shorthold tenancies”, but these will automatically convert when the law is in force.

An Airbnb is for temporary stays that should not qualify as a tenant’s main accommodation, so it is exempt from this legislation.

No, the Renters’ Rights Act is just for residential properties – however, in cases where a property is mixed use (a flat above a shop, for example), then it will apply to the flat.

In England, the new law states that you cannot serve notice for repossession until 12 months after the start of the tenancy agreement. If you have an existing tenancy that’s already longer than 12 months, you should be free to serve notice on the grounds of wanting to sell. With the Section 21 abolished, you’ll need to serve a section 8 notice.

Note there are some grounds for repossession where you’ll need to give the tenant prior notice – such as evicting students in order to allow new students to move in at the start of the academic year. You can check the Government’s grounds for possession guidance online.

data.title: I visited Britain’s most battered train bridge. No wonder the railways are in chaos
data.description:
For people living near Watling Street in Hinckley, it’s not unusual to be woken in the night by a thunderous bang as another lorry comes to grief at the local railway bridge.

This small Leicestershire town is home to Britain’s most battered bridge. Surrounded by depots of enormous vehicles, it was struck at least 22 times by lorries and buses throughout 2024 and 2025.

Across the country, a railway bridge is hit by a road vehicle every five hours, with about 1,700 strikes on bridges recorded by Network Rail between April 2024 and March 2025.

The result was 120 days of delays to train journeys last year and a £23m bill for taxpayers, with each strike costing about £13,000.

Some 43 per cent of lorry drivers admit to not measuring their vehicle before setting off and 52 per cent do not take low bridges into account.

Outside Hinckley on the A5, heavy goods vehicles of all types, from concrete mixers to delivery vans, push their luck at speed, zooming eye-wateringly close to the underbelly of the bridge that bears the scars of past collisions.

Each time one crunches underneath the steelwork, mayhem ensues, with long traffic jams, train delays and debris littering the road, according to residents.

“It’s a nightmare,” one neighbour says. “It’s frightening, like a bang or an explosion. It happens all the time, at least once a week.”

The bridge’s luminous yellow-striped warning signs are visibly scarred by scrapes, while the supporting walls are missing large chunks of brick.

In the lay-by, splinters and vehicle parts lie scattered through the undergrowth and residents of a nearby caravan park report tables, chairs and even mattresses flying out of lorries.

A mother with two young daughters sighs as she recalls how the bangs shake the caravan almost weekly.

Chantelle Callton, 35, tells The Telegraph: “Oh my God, it shakes. It’s like a bomb’s gone off. Everybody speeds down here, so it’s very dangerous.”

The bridge is so notorious that she was warned about it more than six years ago before she moved there.

“If we go a week when it’s not been hit, we’ll say: ‘We’re due one soon,’” she adds.

Dianna Smith, 60, who runs the caravan site with her husband, says her son and his friend had to help a trapped driver out of a lorry in the night. “It’s a ticking time-bomb until something bad happens,” she adds.

“They get that much speed up and don’t realise they’re too high. One lorry’s top was pulled back like a tin opener. They have to stop the trains and it messes everything up.”

Betty Watton, 44, sitting outside with her two chihuahuas, lives closest to the bridge and says drivers often flee the scene.

She says: “One lorry tore the whole top off and just left it in the road, rolled up like a big piece of scrap. One time paint tins spilt and paint went everywhere, but it’s mainly parcels.”

At a nearby depot filled with HGVs and lorries, workers clad in orange hi-vis laugh knowingly at the mention of the bridge.

They refuse to speak, but one jokes to another: “How many times have you hit it?”

Vehicles over a certain height at the Greenway Environmental depot have to take lengthy detours to avoid the bridge, including driving on to the motorway, the assistant manager says.

Another worker suggests it might not be the most strategic location for a lorry depot.

“Earlier this year, one was full of papers, cardboard and packaging. When that got ripped apart, it peeled the lid back and paper and card flew everywhere, blocking the street off for quite a while,” he says.

“There are a lot of industrial sites and major warehouses here. The kind of places that have drivers that are coming here for the very first time. They follow their sat nav, but still...

“There are a lot of light-up signs the whole way saying: ‘High vehicle. Turn back.’ But it’s become quite an issue that lorry drivers use their phones while driving.”

Mountpark, which is funding the project and working with National Highways, has planned to lower the road under the bridge since 2023, but critics of the scheme fear it would be complex and lead to flooding.

Karen, the landlady of a nearby pub, says road works will only add to the traffic jams blighting her business.

With the construction of a new supermarket depot around the bridge, she fears that the problem will not be going away anytime soon.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Tories promise to abolish 24-hour bus lanes
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to abolish 24-hour bus lanes to ease traffic congestion.

The party is launching their six-point plan for motorists before the local elections on May 7 with a promise of “bus lanes that make sense”.

Kemi Badenoch, the Tory leader, said they would restrict the operating hours of bus lanes to allow cars to use them more frequently.

Mrs Badenoch said Labour wanted “to make drivers’ lives as difficult as possible”. In recent years, the number of bus lanes operating 24 hours a day, seven days a week, has soared in cities across the Midlands and Merseyside.

In London, more than 50 miles of bus lanes became a 24-hour operation in 2021 under Heidi Alexander, the former deputy mayor for transport and now the transport secretary.

Under Tory plans, all 24/7 bus lanes will be scrapped unless a government minister provides specific written permission for one to remain open.

Conservative sources pointed to a Tory council in West Northamptonshire scrapping a 24-hour bus lane, making it only operational between 7.30am and 9.30am.

Manchester city council issued the highest number of fines for bus lane violations, at more than 500,000 between 2023 and 2025.

According to data from used-car firm Cinch, Bristol city council came second at 344,000 fines, followed by Sheffield city council at 275,360.

The implementation and operation of bus lanes is down to individual councils to decide what is best for their area.

Government guidance says bus lanes “should be provided only when they are needed” and operate “only when buses are running” or when traffic is sufficiently busy to cause delays.

It adds that local authorities should make sure that if a bus lane does operate at quieter times of the day, there is a strong case for it and the effect on other users is “mitigated”.

The Tories’ six-point plan for motorists also includes a promise to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Tory councils will review all 20mph measures, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The Conservatives have also promised to scrap the rise in fuel duty planned by the Chancellor for September, and unroll a “national pothole patrol” to fix Britain’s roads.

The party also said it would recruit more driving test examiners and increase training capacity to reduce the backlog, which has risen by more than three weeks under Labour.

A Labour spokesman said: “Nobody should let the Tories behind the wheel again. After 14 years in charge, they left our roads riddled with potholes and the driving test system in chaos, with waiting times spiralling on their watch.”

data.title: Have-a-go heroes not the answer to shoplifting problem, says John Lewis boss
data.description:
The boss of John Lewis has dismissed the outbreak of public support for have-a-go heroes who tackle shoplifters, saying they are not the solution to the crime wave gripping the high street.

Jason Tarry, the chairman of the high street giant, said the shoplifting wave was the worst he had seen “in my 35 years in retail”.

Despite receiving “daily reports of abuse, organised criminal gangs and serious physical attacks on John Lewis and Waitrose employees”, the answer was not to “encourage” workers to take on thieves, he said.

The John Lewis chairman said law enforcement was failing to do enough to address the issue. His intervention came just days after Sir Keir Starmer said the “tide could be turning” on shoplifting, after a 17pc rise in people being charged and a 1pc fall in the number of incidents.

Mr Tarry’s comments also follow the recent sacking of a Waitrose employee for apprehending a shoplifter. The supermarket chain is part of the John Lewis Partnership.

Walker Smith, who has worked for 17 years as a shop assistant at a Waitrose branch in Clapham Junction, south-west London, claims he was sacked after confronting a thief who was attempting to steal Easter eggs.

In the aftermath of his dismissal, Waitrose said: “There is a serious danger to life in tackling shoplifters. We refuse to put anyone’s life at risk and that’s why we have policies in place that are very clearly understood and must be strictly followed.”

However, political figures and others rallied behind Mr Smith. Lord Walker, Iceland’s chairman and Labour’s cost of living tsar, offered him a job at the frozen-food chain.

Lord Walker called for security guards to carry truncheons and pepper spray after a gang of youths invaded a Marks & Spencer store in Clapham.

Writing for The Telegraph, Mr Tarry said John Lewis security staff “will intervene to challenge shoplifters – but only if they’ve been trained and it’s safe to do so”.

The partnership’s policy runs counter to trade union advice to security guards, who are being told not to stop shoplifters over health and safety fears.

The Security Industry Federation has told its members: “Do not put yourselves in harm’s way for those who may not support you afterwards.”

Mr Tarry also rejected suggestions that retailers were “a soft touch – leaving an open door for the criminals”. “I can reassure you that this couldn’t be further from the truth,” he said.

The industry has ramped up investment recently in guards, tagging, physical barriers, CCTV and body cameras to protect workers, customers and businesses from theft.

With the financial hit from lost goods factored in, the cost for the John Lewis ran into the “many millions”, he added.

Mr Tarry argues that law enforcement ought to do more to address the shoplifting surge in Britain.

Annual shoplifting offences in England and Wales passed 500,000 for the first time last year, according to official figures. However, retailers believe the data drastically underestimate the extent of the problem.

“A bolder, collective approach” involving high-street shops, the police and the courts is required “to make a difference”, he said.

The passing of the Crime and Policing Act into Royal Assent marks a step forward on what Mr Tarry called a “collective mission” to tackle shoplifting and physical attacks on shop employees.

Under the new legislation, it will become a standalone criminal offence in England and Wales to assault a retail worker.

The Government’s decision to scrap rules treating stolen goods worth less than £200 as “low-value shoplifting” was a welcome move because it was “limiting” investigations, Mr Tarry said.

But he said courts need “to more consistently use the sentencing tools available to them to bring criminals to justice and help their rehabilitation”.

Mr Tarry added: “To coin a phrase, we want the criminal justice system to be tough on crime and the causes of crime by encouraging the proper use of the new criminal offence of assaulting a retail worker and the greater use of Criminal Behaviour Orders by the courts.”

He also warned shoppers to prepare for the deployment of more intrusive technology to combat the problem: “We want to invest in even better technology to beat the criminals. Like other retailers, we’re considering the benefits of facial recognition technology.”

Addressing concerns about privacy, consent and accuracy, Mr Tarry said cameras are becoming more sophisticated so that they “target only prolific offenders, ignore innocent bystanders and eradicate human error”.

We need a bolder response to the retail crime wave

By Jason Tarry

In my 35 years in retail, I have never seen such a high level of retail crime as I see on the shop floor today. I receive daily reports of low-level abuse, organised criminal gangs and serious physical attacks on John Lewis and Waitrose employees.

I share the anger and frustration that honest shop workers feel unsafe in their place of work, but I don’t believe the solution to the retail crime wave is to encourage have-a-go heroes or to lay the responsibility solely at the feet of retailers.

Instead, a bolder, collective approach is needed by retailers, legislators and law enforcement to tackle the problem. Wednesday’s passing of the Crime and Policing Act marks a step forward on that collective mission.

The John Lewis Partnership has long supported the introduction of a standalone criminal offence in England and Wales for assaulting a retail worker and we welcome the scrapping of the £200 threshold, which was limiting the investigation of low-level thefts.

These are clear signals that legislators are willing to stand shoulder to shoulder with retailers to tackle the problem, but we all know that more is needed to make a difference.

First, we need to reinforce the working partnerships that exist between retailers and police. Second, we need to fully utilise new technology to gather and share intelligence with law enforcement. Third, we need courts to more consistently use the sentencing tools available to them to bring criminals to justice and help their rehabilitation.

It’s been said that retailers are a soft touch, leaving an open door for the criminals. I can reassure you that this couldn’t be further from the truth. The cost to retailers of protecting their workers, customers and businesses from theft runs into many millions in safeguarding measures – guards, tagging, physical barriers, CCTV, body cameras – not to mention the cost of lost stock.

This takes away money from lowering prices for customers, investing in our employees or improving our stores. In our employee-owned business, it’s even more infuriating as our employees are literally watching their profits being stolen from them.

And to be clear, our people will intervene to challenge shoplifters – but only if they’ve been trained and it’s safe to do so. This is the right approach supported by the police, many retailers and the British Retail Consortium. No shop leader wants to have to make the call to a family member to say their loved one has been hurt, or worse, trying to stop a shoplifter. Nothing we sell is worth the safety of our workers.

So the only way forward is clear: a continued investment in a bolder collective approach by retailers, lawmakers and law enforcement.

In our John Lewis and Waitrose shops, we will continue to grow the trusted partnerships we have with police officers in our communities – hosting surgeries in shops and welcoming them to drop into our canteens for a free drink or subsidised meal.

We already work with many police forces to share intelligence and evidence gathered in our shops using CCTV and body-camera footage. This enables police forces across the country, including the Met, to pool intelligence from different retailers to track and prosecute prolific offenders. We need all police forces across the country to adopt this intelligence-sharing approach with retailers for even greater impact.

We want to invest in even better technology to beat the criminals. Like other retailers, we’re considering the benefits of facial recognition technology. We understand the debates around the use of this technology, but systems are improving in sophistication to target only prolific offenders, ignore innocent bystanders and eradicate human error.

We can help to increase the public’s understanding of how customer privacy can be respected while employing the right tools and practices to keep our shops and communities safe.

Lastly, to coin a phrase, we want the criminal justice system to be tough on crime and the causes of crime by encouraging the proper use of the new criminal offence of assaulting a retail worker and the greater use of criminal behaviour orders by the courts. 

Where appropriate, criminal behaviour orders can be used as a rehabilitation tool, for example, providing treatment for addictions, to try to prevent reoffending.

The new law is a step in the right direction and gives confidence that politicians believe that shop workers’ livelihoods – and lives – are worth protecting.

Now, the challenge is to make sure that the broader collective approach that I have championed here is taken forward. We, the John Lewis Partnership, are ready to play our part.

Jason Tarry is chairman of the John Lewis Partnership

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.

data.title: FT journalists rebel over back-to-office order
data.description:
Journalists at the Financial Times are mounting a rebellion over efforts to order staff back to the office.

Members of the National Union of Journalists (NUJ) have unanimously voted to launch a dispute procedure over plans to introduce a four-day office week.

The union said bosses had “not made a compelling case” for increasing the mandate from its current level of three days, and raised a series of concerns about the new policy.

These included fears that the measures could discriminate against parents, in particular mothers, and would leave many staff members out of pocket because of commuting costs.

The union also warned that some employees had been hired on the basis of a three-day office week.

The policy is only thought to apply to journalists at the FT’s London headquarters and not to commercial staff or those based in other offices around the globe.

In an email to members, first reported by The Guardian, NUJ officials said: “The FT chapel believes that the edict comes at a time when, more than ever, our coverage depends on the goodwill and flexibility of editorial staff.”

Under the terms of the FT’s house agreement with the NUJ, the union can ballot for strike action if the dispute process fails to lead to an agreement.

An FT spokesman said: “We’re discussing newsroom office attendance with the NUJ.”

Staff anger over the change in policy has been growing since Roula Khalaf, the editor, emailed employees at the end of March to complain that some had been falling “well short” of the three-day mandate.

Ms Khalaf announced plans to move to a four-day week instead, with those wishing to work from home more often told to write to the managing editor’s office to seek approval.

Steve Bird, the NUJ’s representative at the FT, said there had been a “very strong reaction” to the announcement among staff.

Concerns about executive pay

The FT, which is owned by Japanese media giant Nikkei, has had a number of run-ins with the union in recent years over pay for its top executives.

John Ridding, the previous chief executive, was forced to return a chunk of his £2.6m salary in 2018 after a group of journalists at the title complained about his pay.

The latest accounts for the newspaper show the highest-paid executive took home just more than £1.8m in 2024, as global revenues rose 6pc to £540m.

Revenues at the FT’s UK operations rose 2pc but operating profit fell 19pc, which bosses blamed on inflation and expanding the workforce by 30 extra employees.

The union last year secured a two-year pay deal after months of negotiations. John Slade, who was previously the company’s chief commercial officer, took over as chief executive last summer.

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: Britain in talks to sell Rolls-Royce mini nuclear reactors to Sweden
data.description:
Britain is in advanced talks about a deal that would see Rolls-Royce supply pioneering mini nuclear power plants to Sweden.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, said the discussions with Stockholm were among several taking place between the Government and European allies as part of efforts to strengthen energy security in light of the Iran war.

Rolls-Royce’s small modular reactors (SMRs) are seen as a breakthrough because they could be produced in factories and then assembled on site, dramatically reducing build times compared to larger plants.

The first ones are set to be built in the UK and the Czech Republic, but Mr Kyle said he was currently touring European capitals in an effort to secure further orders.

The Business Secretary told The Telegraph: “I’m in advanced talks with Sweden. I’m also beating the drum with other European countries and further afield.

“I see this as central to our growth agenda back home. It’s about the re-industrialisation of the British economy, placing it at the forefront of highly innovative, precise and growth-orientated, highly profitable manufacturing.”

It comes after Rolls-Royce won a design competition run by Great British Energy – Nuclear last year.

That will see Rolls-Royce supply the mini reactors for three power plants being built in Anglesey, North Wales, with the potential room for another five on the site.

The company has also signed an agreement to build another six in Temelín for the Czech state energy company ČEZ, while in Sweden it is in the final running to supply its SMR technology to state energy giant Vattenfall.

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, said the Czech deal would unlock “a significant programme of work”.

Mr Kyle said he was now pushing for the Derby-based engineering giant to secure further work on the Continent as the Middle East crisis prompted fresh worries about energy security.

Those potentially interested could even include Germany, where large nuclear plants have been phased out but ministers have recently expressed an interest in SMRs.

Mr Kyle is poised to travel to Berlin next week and is expected to raise the subject during his visit.

Last year, The Telegraph revealed Sweden had approached allies including the UK about the potential to place a bulk order for SMRs.

Ebba Busch, the Swedish deputy prime minister, said her country wanted to band together to buy at least 10 to 15 reactors in an effort to cut costs and share expertise.

Rolls-Royce has said it can get the cost per SMR down to an estimated $3bn (£2.2bn) per unit once production is up and running, although the technology remains unproven.

Tufan Erginbilgic, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce, has estimated that the world will need 400 SMRs by 2050 and that the British company has a chance to dominate the market.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BT makes big bet it can revive battered brand
data.description:
BT is to spend tens of millions of pounds attempting to restore its brand this summer as the company tries to realise its growth ambitions.

The former state monopoly will revive the BT Mobile brand alongside a major advertising campaign with the slogan “brilliant things”.

It marks a crucial milestone in the efforts of Allison Kirkby, the chief executive, to restore the BT brand to prominence after her predecessors concluded it was too badly damaged by years of poor customer service, and was struggling with younger consumers.

BT stopped accepting new customers on BT Mobile and made EE its flagship consumer brand in 2023, before the 58-year-old Scot arrived in early 2024.

Ms Kirkby’s plan to emphasise BT’s critical importance to the UK has been agreed with the company’s biggest shareholder, Sunil Bharti Mittal, the Indian billionaire who owns a stake of almost 25pc. He invested in the company partly on the belief in its role as a national champion.

BT will unveil the changes at an event at Wembley Stadium next week alongside football stars. The company has a significant sponsorship deal with the England football team through EE. The brand’s logo appears on the team’s kits and around the stadium.

The company has had a shifting relationship with sport after making an expensive attempt to challenge Sky with the launch of BT Sport in 2013, which has since been renamed TNT Sports. But it has since backed away from the venture and made an aborted attempt to sell its stake last year.

BT Mobile is to be relaunched following BT’s withdrawal from the mobile market. It used to offer mobile services with its discount Plusnet brand.

Most growth among the mobile providers is accounted for by so-called virtual operators such as Plusnet, who typically charge less, rather than network owners including EE.

Under Philip Jansen, the previous boss, the company outlined plans to ditch “BT” as the flagship brand for millions of mobile and broadband customers and instead focus on the EE name. It also announced a major push into consumer electronics, such as games consoles offered under the EE brand.

But Ms Kirkby shelved the plans amid concerns that dropping BT could alienate older customers, while she has sought to refocus the company on its core connectivity offering.

BT has been battling to reverse a reputation for poor customer service over the last decade. Its brands received the most Ofcom complaints for broadband and landline services in 2016, but its scores have improved in recent years after call centres were moved back to the UK.

In addition to bolstering its eponymous brand, BT is hoping to strengthen its position in an increasingly competitive market.

The £15bn merger of Vodafone and Three has created a major new rival to EE and triggered an explosion of smaller new services known as mobile virtual network operators (MVNOs), which piggyback off the major players. Monzo, Revolut and Lidl are among the brands plotting new low-cost mobile offerings.

The advertising campaign, which coincides with BT’s 180th anniversary, is aimed at emphasising the company’s heritage and its key role as a provider of critical national infrastructure amid growing political concern about sovereignty. BT last summer tapped the advertising agency Uncommon to oversee the brand refresh.

BT’s campaign will attempt to recapture some of the public affection of its 1990s “It’s good to talk” ads featuring Bob Hoskins, as well as its 1980s ads starring Maureen Lipman.

It comes at a critical time for Ms Kirkby who has been under pressure to return BT to growth after a radical period of change, even as its share price has risen with market trends.

She has slashed thousands of jobs after promising to strip out £3bn of costs, while she has replaced the entire executive team.

BT’s underlying growth has stalled and the company has been haemorrhaging broadband customers to scrappy start-ups known as alt-nets. Its shares have also underperformed some other telecoms companies.

Compounding matters is pressure from Mr Mittal, who has been pushing Ms Kirkby to pursue her growth plans more aggressively. Speculation is also growing in the industry that the tycoon may install his 38-year-old son Shravin in a senior executive role, though a source close to Mr Mittal denied this.

It comes as Ofcom launched an investigation into BT on Wednesday over concerns it had failed to comply with information requests.

The regulator said it was concerned that the company had provided incomplete or inaccurate information from EE and Plusnet in response to a request for data about landline and broadband customers in late 2023.

data.title: How to buy and let a holiday home in France
data.description:
This is part of a series of guides on buying and letting property abroad.

Are you the landlord of an overseas property? We’d like to hear about your experience – email money@telegraph.co.uk*

If you’re considering buying an overseas holiday home and want to rent it out, France continues to be a popular choice.

British holidaymakers might be renowned for their poor efforts at speaking the language, but they travel there in their droves regardless. In 2024, France welcomed 9.3 million UK visitors, according to the latest government figures, lured by the country’s beautiful scenery, quaint villages and cosmopolitan cities (not to mention a penchant for wine and cheese). 

This makes it the UK’s second most popular holiday destination, second only to Spain.

But buying a property in France isn’t as straightforward as snapping up a rental property in the UK.

Here, we speak to a number of French mortgage, legal and tax experts to find out what you need to know.

  • How to find the best area
  • What kind of yields can you expect?
  • How easy is it to get a mortgage?
  • What are the tax implications?
  • Are there any pitfalls to be aware of?

How to find the best area

If you’ve been holidaying regularly in France over the years, you’ll probably have a pretty good idea where you want to buy.

But if you want to rent the property – either exclusively or just when you’re not using it – you’ll also want to make sure it will appeal to a broad range of tourists and offer good value for money in a competitive market.

If you don’t have a massive budget, Judith Whitlow, director at Beaux Villages Immobilier, a Savills associate, said the trick is to look beyond the big-name destinations. “That’s where you find the real charm and better value,” she said.

“Take Bellac in the Haute Vienne. It’s a proper market town with cafés, restaurants, summer festivals and even a mainline station. You can stroll into town in the morning for a coffee and a baguette, and be at Limoges airport in half an hour. Yet you can still pick up a two-bedroom cottage from around €70,000 (£60,650). Holidaymakers love the authenticity, and owners appreciate the realistic price point.”

She also reported growing interest in Charente. “It’s a part of France that still feels very genuine: rolling vineyards, pretty river towns and locals who always have time for a chat at the market.”

“It has excellent transport links, but without the high expense of its famous neighbour, the Dordogne. Families especially love the space and slower pace of life, and for under €200,000 you can still find detached farmhouses with land that let well both in peak season and during those shoulder months when couples and retirees travel.”

Simon Conn, a specialist overseas mortgage broker, added that while coastal locations and alpine ski resorts will likely attract interest from renters, they also compare favourably to city rentals, including Paris, Marseille and Nice.

What kind of yields can you expect?

The average rental yield across France was 4.84pc in the final quarter of 2025, according to the Global Property Guide. If you’re planning to allow holidaymakers to rent your property on a short-term basis, you would probably hope for higher, but Mr Conn said it’s important to be realistic.

“The yield is difficult to define because it depends on whether the let will be long-term or seasonal. A rough indication could be 5pc, but it really depends on several variables – amount of time, management costs and so on.”

He added: “The weekly and monthly short-term seasonal rental yields are higher, but only for the season and not the full year, and the management costs can be higher.”

Ms Whitlow agreed: “Yields will always vary. A simple village house won’t bring the same return as a larger home with a pool and views, but well-presented properties in the right spot are letting strongly.”

How easy is it to get a mortgage?

Buying a property outright will always be more straightforward. Even if you don’t have the ready cash, you might be able to remortgage against a UK property to raise the necessary finance.

However, that’s not to say it’s impossible to get a mortgage in France. The key is to get specialist advice.

“There are relatively few French lenders who will lend to UK residents,” said Miranda John, director of international property at mortgage broker SPF Private Clients. “These lenders are looking for the property to be in a prime location, and the minimum loan is €300,000.

“Mortgages are virtually always on a repayment basis, and loan-to-values (LTVs) can be generous – up to 80pc – but for property with a value of €2m or higher, this usually falls to 70-75pc. French banks generally prefer to offer a fixed rate for the term of the mortgage.”

She added that the amount you can borrow could be less if you’re borrowing from a smaller lender: “LTVs can vary considerably depending on where you buy, and if the property is too rural, finance may not be available to a non-resident via a local lender.”

If you’re letting a property out, Mr Conn pointed out that the affordability assessments are different from those in the UK. “It is important to note that the French banks do not offer ‘self-financing’ buy-to-let mortgages, despite the intention to let the property,” he said.

“The applicant must fit the mortgage affordability criteria based on their personal and/or professional income and expenses – mortgage, rent, loans, and so on.”

What are the tax implications?

If you’re letting out a property in France but still live in the UK, you’ll need to declare the rental income on both sides of the channel. But that doesn’t necessarily mean you get taxed twice.

Sally Dilks, a senior associate at Buckles Law, said: “Under the 2008 Double Tax Treaty between the UK and France covering income tax (among others), in principle any such French tax paid is allowed as a credit against any UK tax computed by reference to the same income, but this is a specialist area, and so expert advice should be sought from a bilingual, dually qualified accountant.”

You’ll also need to budget for French stamp duty or transfer tax. “For existing properties, this is likely to be at the recently increased rate of 6.31pc currently applicable to property purchases in most departments of France. It forms the majority of the French notary’s fees, with the balance being their fees and disbursements,” she added.

This tax works a little differently if you’re buying off-plan. “For off-plan properties, a reduced rate of stamp duty is payable because VAT (currently at the rate of 20pc in most areas) must also be paid,” said Ms Dilks.

Mr Conn said you’ll also need to factor in “taxe d’habitation” if you don’t occupy the property yourself. This will be “determined by the size, accommodation, services, and location of the property”.

In addition to tax costs you’ll need to consider when you buy or let the property, it’s also wise to think about taxes that could be payable when you sell it or die.

Ms Dilks explained that there is also a double tax treaty for capital gains, but warned that the French equivalent of the tax is complicated and that if you are selling a property worth more than €150,000, you’ll need to use an accredited French fiscal representative to calculate the bill.

She said: “To avoid paying the higher rate of the ‘social charges’ element of French capital gains tax, they must ensure they get a certificate from HMRC confirming that for the tax year of sale, they are affiliated to the UK social security regime.

“As this can take several weeks to obtain, to avoid complications on completion of the sale, they should ensure they apply for the certificate as soon as a sale has been agreed.”

The situation on inheritance is also complicated by the fact that the French equivalent of the tax – succession tax or “droits de succession” – doesn’t work in the same way as in the UK.

“The main thing to bear in mind is that rather than a nil-rate band being applicable to a deceased’s entire French estate and a flat rate of IHT thereafter – as in the UK – in France, different tax-free allowances, exemptions and IHT rates are applied to each category of beneficiary.

”[This depends] on the nature of their relationship to the deceased – for example, spouse/civil partner, child (biological or adopted, but not stepchild), siblings, nephews and nieces, unmarried partner/stepchild/friend, and so on.”

As such, it’s once again essential to make sure you get specialist cross-border tax advice if you’re likely to be impacted.

Are there any pitfalls to be aware of?

Rules and regulations will vary depending on the location of the property in France, which could cause unwelcome surprises for some British property owners.

“Bear in mind that many towns and cities in France have taken action to preserve dwellings for their residents,” said Ms Dilks.

“Renting out your principal residence (including on platforms such as Airbnb or Booking.com) is limited to a maximum of 120 days a year and is subject to prior registration, plus the payment of a tourist tax.”

If you’re considering a longer-term let, you might also be impacted by rental controls if demand for property outstrips supply. These areas are referred to as a “zone tendue”, which translates as “tense zones”. There may be caps on the number of rental properties permitted in these areas, as well as possible additional taxes for properties such as second homes that are unoccupied.

Ms Dilks added: “To check if your property is in a ‘zone tendue’, which is likely to be the case in cities such as Paris, Lyon and Bordeaux, you should make enquiries at the local town hall.”

If the property you are buying is in an apartment block, such as a “copropriété”, you will also need to check the management’s regulations to ensure that letting is permitted. “For example, short-term lets in desirable areas may be discouraged due to the risk of nuisance to resident owners or long-term tenants,” said Ms Dilks.

In some cases, it may be necessary to get a rental licence. Mr Conn said: “Certain locations in France restrict the number of properties in that area that can be marketed on short-term rental platforms (like Airbnb), and in these cases, it is necessary to have a licence from the commune to let the property on such a platform.”

Ms Dilks also warned that while it’s still possible to buy off-plan rental properties using the leaseback scheme (which provides a VAT exemption in exchange for an agreement to let the property on a commercial lease for 20 years), they can be problematic.

“Such schemes are strictly regulated, and commercial tenants are given a lot of protection, so it may be difficult and expensive to terminate the lease,” she said.

In fact, some British property owners who used the scheme to buy ski chalets have reported six-figure exit penalties, so it’s vital to understand the agreement you’re signing up for.

Finally, as with any overseas purchase, Mr Conn stressed the importance of getting your own building survey completed, checking the translation of relevant documents and ensuring you have an opt-out clause in case any loan falls through.
