data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Security guards told not to stop shoplifters
data.description:
Security guards are being told not to stop shoplifters over health and safety fears despite a crime wave sweeping the country.

Union bosses are telling their members to “watch, report and be an expert witness for police”, but are explicitly ordering them not to intervene against theft.

The Security Industry Federation (SIF) has told its members, “do not put yourselves in harm’s way for those who may not support you afterwards”.

The union’s general secretary Daniel Garnham said that while this diktat went “against the instincts and principles of many good security professionals... someone else’s stock is not more important than your safety, your career or your licence”.

He added: “Security workers cannot continue carrying all of the personal and professional risk while others avoid responsibility.”

The retail union Usdaw, which has 360,000 members, is also telling its members “not to intervene against potentially dangerous criminals and to keep themselves safe”.

GMB – one of Britain’s biggest unions representing thousands of security guards – said that until better regulation was in place, “retailers cannot expect security guards to put themselves at physical risk just to protect their bottom lines”.

Retailers are facing a shoplifting epidemic in Britain, with offences surging by 133pc over the past four years on the back of a cost of living crisis.

Supermarkets have come under fire for so-called “deter-not-detain” policies designed to keep staff safe, with videos circulating on social media showing criminals walking out of shops unchallenged.

Waitrose sacked a member of staff who had worked for the supermarket for 17 years after he stopped a suspected shoplifter stealing a bag of luxury Easter eggs, it emerged this month.

Morrisons was also criticised for firing one of its store managers after he tackled a prolific thief last week in breach of the supermarket’s policy.

The unions’ advice is thought to be the first widespread instance of security guards officially being told not to take action.

It threatens to undermine Sir Keir Starmer’s efforts to clamp down on rising crime as he seeks to reset his premiership in the wake of the Mandelson scandal and in the run-up to the local elections.

The SIF explained it had adopted its stance and will retain it until the supermarket industry comes up with a unified policy on how to tackle shoplifters.

Mr Garnham said guards were “expected to deter theft, protect staff and reassure the public” but that workers at different shops were being given different instructions.

“Across the UK there is no joined-up approach,” he said, which was putting guards in “an impossible position”.

“Security workers moving between different stores and contracts are often expected to work to different rules in different places. In one site they may be told to observe and report only. In another they may be expected to stop and detain offenders. In another they may face disciplinary action for trying to intervene at all.

“This is unfair on workers, unsafe for the public and completely unacceptable in a profession that plays such an important role in keeping people safe.”

He added that he had written to supermarkets “to push for a joined-up approach and to ensure clear and consistent instructions would be put in place across the board”.

Meanwhile, GMB said it has been trying to raise industry standards for a year with the Security Industry Authority (SIA), the arm’s-length government regulator for the security sector, but claimed it has been ignored.

Guidance from the SIA on intervening against shoplifters says guards “do not have any special powers that extend beyond the powers of any other citizen”.

It says “security operatives may use such force as is reasonable in the circumstances in the prevention of crime or the citizen’s arrest of an offender”, but adds that any force used “must be necessary to the circumstances and proportionate to the threat”.

The Conservatives criticised the unions’ stance amid the rising crime wave under Labour.

Chris Philp MP, shadow home secretary, said “security unions telling their members to stand back and watch thieves walk out” was “the epitome of what Labour’s breakdown in law and order looks like”.

“Shoplifting is up 8pc since Labour took office; a security worker was murdered in a shopping centre in February; guards are being told their employer won’t back them if they intervene, so they don’t intervene; and Starmer is abolishing prison sentences under a year, which means virtually no shoplifter will ever go to prison,” he said.

Zia Yusuf, Reform UK’s shadow home secretary, said criminals were “facing little to no consequence” for their crimes, saying: “In Labour’s Britain, shoplifting has become a form of shopping.”

“Protecting shop workers from violence and intimidation means getting serious on crime. Police must attend every reported theft, and repeat offenders should face real custodial sentences,” he said.

“Crime must have consequences.”

The Prime Minister claimed on Monday that “the tide could be turning” against shoplifting as he set out new laws to reduce spiralling crime.

In an address to the Usdaw shopworkers’ union, Sir Keir said: “It’s disgraceful that people just working in their shop have to take abuse from customers.

“It’s disgraceful that people feel sick to the stomach thinking about how they’re going to get through the day, and it’s disgraceful that people can have their lives and livelihoods ruined by persistent shop theft.”

He said the Government had put an extra 3,000 neighbourhood police officers on the streets and scrapped the “ridiculous” rule which left theft of goods worth less than £200 “not properly investigated” by police.

“That was a shoplifters’ charter, and we’ve ended it and not before time,” he said.

“We’ve toughened up punishment too. We’re giving police stronger powers, making the abuse and assault of retail workers a specific crime and giving you the same protections as emergency workers.”

Sir Keir said he was “not blind to how big this challenge is” but said the number of people charged had gone up 17pc in the latest statistics and shop theft was down.

The latest Office for National Statistics data show shoplifting offences fell slightly last year, down from 516,611 in 2024 to 509,566 in 2025.

“It’s only slightly down, but the tide could be turning,” Sir Keir claimed.

Lucy Whing, crime policy lead at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retail crime remains a major issue, and retailers have invested £5bn over the past five years to help tackle this issue, with security guards playing a key role in preventing crime. Retailers are continuing to work closely with police, government and the security industry to tackle the scourge of theft and violence.”

The SIA said: “Individual retailers set their own policies and procedures on how they detect, combat or respond to shoplifters.”

data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

This included a first-class journey and an anytime return.

The discount brought the cost of the first-class ticket below the price of standard class, making it allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy.

The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking a journey with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for another journey.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: Tories pledge to drop blanket 20mph speed limits
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said on Thursday that Left-wing councils have turned 20mph into the “national speed of bureaucracy”.

Vowing they would be eliminated by Conservative-led councils, he said: “We are ending blanket limits that slow whole towns to a crawl while doing little for real safety.

“Town halls have spent years waging a war on the very people who get up early, drive to work and keep the economy turning.

“Only the Conservatives are prepared to end the war on drivers, rip up the blanket bans, and get Britain moving again for a stronger economy.”

Existing 20mph measures will be reviewed, Mr Holden said, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The announcement comes before the council elections on May 7.

Labour-run Wales is the main example of a blanket 20mph speed limit being imposed on Britain’s drivers.

The devolved government in Cardiff introduced and then partially abandoned a blanket 20mph policy on all local roads. It would become the single most unpopular law in the Senedd’s brief history.

It also cost the policy’s architect, Welsh Labour’s Lee Waters, his political career after he resigned in the wake of the backlash to its introduction.

Although Ken Skates, his successor, announced that the blanket ban would be lifted, few councils have raised their speed limits to 30mph. Only in areas outside schools and hospitals must the speed limit remain at 20mph in Wales.

The experiment left half a million people facing fines for breaking the lowered limit, as The Telegraph reported last year.

Current government guidance to councils, last updated in March 2024 – before Labour’s general election victory in July that year – says: “Traffic authorities should use the right speed limits in the right places. They should keep their speed limits under review and only introduce 20mph limits and zones in the right places, over time and with local support in urban areas and built-up village streets that are primarily residential.”

The Conservatives’ Plan for Drivers, published in 2023, set out the principle that 20mph limits should be targeted and evidence-led rather than imposed as “blanket measures”, a party spokesman said.

Mr Holden added: “The previous Conservative government was clear in our guidance that blanket approaches were wrong. Labour ignored it and their councils kept rolling them out in the teeth of public opposition.”

data.title: ‘My husband is in prison but BT won’t let me cancel his TNT Sports subscription’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*

Dear Ruth,

My husband was sentenced to prison in 2024. His crime was committed before I met him, and I knew nothing about it until his arrest. It has been devastating for me.

Since then, I’ve had problems cancelling the TNT Sports package on our BT account. It’s in his name, although I am a named contact and I pay the bills.

BT said that “named contacts” could not change subscriptions but that it would accept a power of attorney. My husband set up an ordinary power of attorney before he went to prison, but BT told me it would only accept a “lasting” power of attorney (LPA).

As a retired lawyer specialising in powers of attorney, I know the “ordinary” type (also known as “general”) should be adequate for any business matters, particularly as it is only needed during the time he is in prison and he is not suffering from any lack of capacity. However, BT doesn’t accept them. I made a complaint, but it was not upheld.

The only way my husband could make an LPA is for a lawyer to be authorised to go into the prison to see him and get the paperwork signed. That would be very expensive and would take many months.

Foolishly, I tried again to reach a solution with BT recently. The £22.31 monthly charge that I’m paying for a service I’m not using was going to increase by £2 a month at the end of March. I spent one hour and 45 minutes on the phone to BT, and was told the only way I could get rid of the sports package was for them to cancel my husband’s account, at a cancellation cost of almost £600, and open an account in my own name.

Very stupidly, I agreed to do this, but then realised he would lose his email account, which is very important as all his legal and business communications come to it. Prisoners can’t access email, but I am monitoring the account with his permission.

So, I tried to undo the cancellation to put everything back where it was, even though it will involve high monthly costs. The person I spoke to managed to cancel the new account in my name, and I was told my husband’s account would be reinstated.

Then, someone from BT’s “retention team” phoned asking to speak to my husband. I explained again why this wasn’t possible. The caller said he could say nothing to me because I was not a named contact on the account, even though everyone else at BT so far has confirmed that I am.

The worry about this is making me ill. I need a telephone and broadband service and the email address is vital. It is horrible having to go through the trauma of talking about a husband in prison every time I try to speak to BT. Every call is such a nightmare that I have to spend days psyching myself up to undergo the ordeal, and every time things just get worse, not better.

Please could you help me?

SJ, Gloucestershire

Dear reader,

I can only imagine the pain of seeing your husband imprisoned, and then having to repeatedly explain his absence to unsympathetic companies like BT.

Firms have previously been criticised for the way they treat relatives of deceased customers (and many have now improved their processes), but it seems that other scenarios, such as dealing with the loved ones of incarcerated customers, are still causing misery.

I hope this column highlights the importance of companies having a framework in place when these distressing situations arise, so that compassionate and practical solutions can be found swiftly.

You deliberately arranged an ordinary power of attorney for your husband. This is similar to an LPA, but crucially, it is for a temporary period. Someone might want to give a friend or family member an ordinary power of attorney to manage their affairs if, for example, they are physically injured (but still mentally sound), are abroad for a long period of time or are in prison.

An LPA, on the other hand, can be used when someone loses the mental capacity to make their own decisions. You didn’t want to create this power of attorney as it might make it difficult for your husband to resume dealing with his affairs when he’s released in a few years’ time.

You told me: “I think because LPAs now have such a high profile, staff are often not trained to recognise and deal with ordinary powers of attorney. BT has not suggested the power of attorney is invalid, only that it chooses not to deal with them.”

You refer to your actions as “foolish” and “stupid”, but you’re being too hard on yourself.

Prisoners can only call specific numbers belonging to friends and family, and not organisations like BT, so it really is up to you to cancel this TV package – and you have been battling for 18 months to make the telecoms giant listen to you. Occasionally, it suggested a solution, and understandably, you followed its advice.

I was furious with BT on your behalf, as I have been with other companies that have failed to put things right despite the customer complaining for well over a year.

(Yes, I’m talking about Barclays and the Post Office, which I detailed in a previous column. And British Gas in another.)

Thankfully, a day after I intervened, BT called you to apologise, and said it would cancel TNT Sports. You were told that a cancellation with 30 days’ notice could have been placed by yourself, and the firm would look at retraining its staff.

As a gesture of goodwill, it said it would refund six months of the sports charges. I urged BT to reconsider, given you have been trying to cancel it for longer than this. It agreed to a refund of charges for a full year and credited your account with £270. After more nagging by me, BT also sent you flowers to apologise.

A BT spokesman said: “We are very sorry that [your reader] had difficulty cancelling TNT Sports on her husband’s account. To acknowledge her experience, we have provided her with a goodwill gesture, and the complaint has been resolved.”

– Ruth

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

data.title: ‘Full force’ of Iran war will hit food prices soon, supermarkets warn
data.description:
Shoppers have been urged to brace for soaring food prices, as supermarkets warned the “full force” of the war in Iran would hit shelves imminently.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) said it would “not be long” before the Middle East conflict starts to ripple into higher grocery prices.

Its warning came as separate figures from the Confederation of British Industry (CBI) revealed that retailers were already under significant pressure, making it unlikely they could absorb cost increases.

The CBI survey showed that significantly more retailers were seeing sales fall than rise. The number of those experiencing declines in sales outnumbered those with growing demand by a margin of 68pc.

Supermarkets are under particular pressure from the war in Iran, with soaring oil and gas prices set to filter through into greenhouse costs for vegetable growers and more expensive feed for pigs and chickens.

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the BRC, said: “While we’re yet to see the full force of the Middle East conflict feeding into consumer prices, it will not be long before it begins to.”

It comes years after Britain was hit by rising grocery prices during the Ukraine conflict. Food and drink inflation climbed to a peak of 19.2pc in March 2023 as poor harvests, higher energy costs and supply chain disruption from Russia’s invasion of Ukraine pushed prices higher.

The BRC warned the war in Iran could mean Britain starts to see inflation pick up over the summer. Food and drink inflation stood at 3.7pc in March, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics. However, this is expected to rise over the course of the year, as food and drink producers’ energy contracts run out and higher costs ripple through the supply chain.

Harvir Dhillon, an economist at the BRC, said: “We expect we will start to see this filter through in the coming months, but are likely not to see the peak in inflation for another 12 months.”

The Food and Drink Federation (FDF) has forecast that food inflation could reach 10pc by the end of the year.

Ms Dickinson said: “Retailers already face mounting cost pressures from domestic policies, with an extra £10bn a year added over the last two years from employment costs, packaging taxes and more.”

It has led to a number of high-profile collapses, including Claire’s Accessories. On Monday, administrators for the jewellery chain said all standalone Claire’s stores across the UK and Ireland would close. More than 1,300 staff were “notified of redundancy”.

However, the BRC chief said these problems for retailers would be “compounded as rising fuel, fertiliser and commodity prices begin to feed into business costs”.

She added that the Government could help to keep shop-price inflation down by making changes to “non-commodity charges”, industry jargon for the plethora of charges, including green levies and grid infrastructure costs, which push up companies’ energy bills.

Supermarket bosses called on the Chancellor earlier this month to look at “domestic policy levers” to cool inflation, including scrapping green levies on energy bills.

Last week, Simon Roberts, the boss of Sainsbury’s, demanded urgent action to prevent grocery price rises, saying: “Now is the time for the Government to really look at what it can do in the food sector to keep those pressures contained as far as possible.”

Fears that Britain is facing a fresh inflation crisis will create a dilemma for the Bank of England’s rate-setters, who meet on Thursday to decide whether to hold or increase borrowing costs.

Markets expect the Bank of England to hold interest rates at 3.75pc but have forecast that rate-setters will raise borrowing costs twice by the end of the year to 4.25pc.

Mike Watkins, the head of retailer and business insight at NielsenIQ, said: “Increased fuel prices are already leading to higher inflation, and we can expect a similar impact in the food and non-food supply chains in the months to come.”

He added that rising inflation is likely to weigh on household spending in the coming months as consumer confidence remains low.

Despite fears of a new bout of inflation from the Iran war, the latest figures from the BRC and NielsenIQ showed that spring discounts resulted in shop price inflation slowing in April.

The BRC said that offers on clothing and furniture had caused shop price inflation to ease to 1pc in the year to April, down from 1.2pc a month earlier.

Food inflation also eased to 3.1pc in the 12 months to April, down from 3.4pc in March, as supermarkets reduced the prices of Easter items such as chocolate.

data.title: Best notice accounts for 2026: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
If you have a notice account, you’ll be required to notify your bank or building society a specified number of days ahead of making a withdrawal, detailing how much you want to withdraw. This notice period, set by the bank, has to pass before your funds are released.

Notice periods can range from seven days to 180, but the most common are 30, 60 and 90 days. It’s important to choose an account with a notice period that suits you, as it can be a long wait to get your money.

In theory, your patience is rewarded with higher interest rates than you would get with easy- or instant-access savings accounts. But this isn’t always the case, so you should always do some research before committing to a new account.

In this guide, Telegraph Money addresses:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best notice accounts for May
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account
  • How do notice accounts work?
  • Are notice accounts worth it?
  • Notice accounts FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best notice accounts for May

Use the table below to discover the best notice savings accounts available.

If you decide you don’t want to wait to withdraw your cash, see our guide to the best easy-access accounts. Opting for one of the best current accounts is another way to earn interest while maintaining full access to your money.

Or, if you’d prefer to lock in a guaranteed rate for the next 12 months or more, don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For a host of tax-efficient savings options, make sure you check our guide to the best cash Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of analysts Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Typically, notice accounts pay a higher rate of interest than easy-access accounts with the benefit of being able to add funds in the account once it’s been opened.

“But an important factor to consider is many providers won’t allow any withdrawals before the end of the notice period and, on the rare occasion earlier access is allowed, may result in a loss of interest.”

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Especially now, when interest rates are declining, notice accounts will typically offer some very attractive rates.

“However, they’re one of the more complicated structures in the savings market. Quite often people don’t realise that they cannot get money out immediately, so they’re saving into a notice account and all of a sudden, they urgently need the money.

“They’re very good for a headline rate, particularly at the moment. But you need to ensure this isn’t money you’re going to need very quickly.”

How do notice accounts work?

Notice accounts work in a very similar way to easy-access accounts; the difference comes when you want to withdraw money.

Instead of requesting a withdrawal that’s paid out instantly, instead you’ll only receive the money after the requisite notice period – as mentioned, this is commonly 30, 60 or 90 days but there are all sorts of options on the market.

Once the notice period is over, your money will be released.

Are notice accounts worth it?

Notice savings accounts can be worth it if you can plan your savings withdrawals well in advance. In theory, you should receive higher interest rates to make up for the inconvenience, and can usually have unlimited access to your funds – granted, after a bit of a wait.

You can also make ongoing deposits. Unlike some accounts that only accept a single initial deposit, notice accounts allow you to continue adding to your savings whenever you like. Generally, savings will still earn interest during the notice period, but this is worth checking.

For new savers, the notice period may help with money management and preventing impulse buying.

Notice accounts FAQs

Yes, you can withdraw money from the account, but you’ll need to wait until the notice period passes to receive it. This commonly varies from 30 to 180 days.

Some accounts may impose limits on the number of withdrawals that can be made, while others may have a minimum deposit requirement you’ll need to bear in mind – it is worth checking the account’s terms and conditions to find out.

The biggest disadvantage of a notice account is having to wait for a set amount of time before being able to access your funds, which could be an issue if you need money at short notice.

data.title: How to choose the best children’s savings accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A children’s savings account works in a very similar to an adult one. You can open many of these accounts for children under the age of 18 with as little as £1. Seeing the balance grow is a good way to teach children financial skills as many accounts allow children over the age of seven to add and withdraw money themselves.

You can choose between an easy or instant-access, fixed term or a regular savings account. Easy or instant-access accounts allow you to withdraw or deposit money at any time and they usually have lower interest rates. Regular savings accounts require you to save every month and the withdrawals may not be as straightforward. They generally pay higher interest, but this can be reduced if you miss a monthly deposit.

In this guide, we will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best children’s savings accounts in May
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an account for your child
  • How to open a children’s savings account
  • Children’s savings account vs Junior Isa
  • Children’s savings accounts FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best children’s savings accounts in May

The table below highlights the best savings accounts you can open for your children.

For more ways to save for your child, check out our guide to the best Junior cash Isas.

If you’re looking for a new home for your own savings, you can find the best tax-efficient options in our guide to cash Isas, while our pick of easy access savings accounts can reveal the best options if you want to add and withdraw money whenever you like.

Alternatively, you may be able to find higher rates if you’re willing to lock your cash away – the top rates are in our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an account for your child

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “There are many children’s savings accounts to choose from, ranging from easy access, regular savers or Junior Isas. They are a good option for parents wishing to save for their child’s future.”

But she reminded savers of the tax implications: “It is worth noting that children are still liable to pay tax and have the same personal allowance as adults and once the child reaches a certain age, usually 18, the account will automatically roll into an adult savings account, where they will have full control.”

Mark Hicks, of stockbroker Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Children’s accounts are brilliant but are not advertised enough. I think the UK still has a big problem in that financial education, particularly in schools, is not on the agenda. So getting a child an account and encouraging them to save from a young age is incredibly important.”

What makes a good children’s savings account?

  • A good savings rate: This might seem obvious, but the money you save for your children will grow and compound at a much faster rate if you can make sure the interest rate is competitive. If possible, it should at least match inflation – otherwise the money will be losing value in real terms.
  • Easy to manage: Consider whether you’ll need the option to make withdrawals, how you want to open and manage the account, and whether there are decent options when your child gets older – for instance, does the provider offer accounts for teenagers to help them manage their money?
  • Suits your savings plans: Many children’s accounts can be opened with £1, and may have fairly low maximum deposits. However, these won’t be suitable if you have a more significant amount to save.
  • Suitable for your child’s age: Watch out for minimum and maximum age requirements as this may affect when you can start saving for your child, and how long you can save for.

How to open a children’s savings account

  1. Choose an account: Weighing up the factors explained above, choose a provider and account that suits you and your child.
  2. Check the small print: Just in case there are any restrictions or details that aren’t obvious, comb the terms and conditions for anything that might make you change your mind.
  3. Go with your opening method of choice: Once you’re happy, go and open the account. Some providers may let you open an account online or you may have to visit a branch or do it by post. Providers may have their own rules for how old a child needs to be to open an account under their own name, and young children, often under eight, typically need a parent to sign and set one up on their behalf. You may need to provide documents such as a birth certificate for the child and your own ID.
  4. Start saving: You may be able to save into your child’s account via online payments or bank transfers. If your child receives money from friends and family, such as on their birthday, you may also be able to get them involved by taking them to a branch to make a cash deposit. Be sure to take note of any deposit restrictions.

Managing your child’s account

Some accounts may let children manage the account themselves from a certain age, depending on whether they were made a signatory when it was opened.

In some cases, even if the child can manage the account, they may need to have a parent or guardian with them when they visit the bank to request changes, or withdraw money.

Children’s accounts usually convert to an adult savings account once the child turns 18, but there is more flexibility before that and you may be able to access the funds if needed.

Are child’s savings accounts taxed?

Children don’t usually have to pay tax on savings interest – but it’s not because they can’t be taxed. Since children don’t usually have any other forms of income, the amount earned from savings interest rarely exceeds the combined amount of their personal allowance and personal savings allowance (PSA).

But if a parent is contributing money into the account and it earns more than £100, this will go towards their own personal savings allowance.

If you are worried about this, another type of child savings account is the Junior Isa (Jisa).

This has a separate annual £9,000 allowance to the adult Isa that can be put into a cash or stocks and shares Isa for children to access after the turn 18. All growth is tax-free – for both children and parents.

Children’s savings account vs Junior Isa

  • Product choice: There are generally more children’s savings account to choose from than Junior Isas, and interest rates can be higher for savings accounts.
  • Tax: Interest or investment growth in Junior Isas is tax-free, but interest earned in a children’s savings account is taxable.
  • Access: No money can be withdrawn from a Junior Isa. With these accounts, the money is locked away until the child becomes an adult. At this point, the account will convert to an adult Isa, and they will get full control of it. Many children’s savings accounts will allow withdrawals, which can be a useful way to teach children about the value of money.
  • Type of account: Children’s savings account just save in cash, but with a Junior Isa parents can choose to save in cash or invest with a stocks and shares option for their child.

Children’s savings accounts FAQs

Grandparents may be able to open a savings account for their grandchildren, as long as they bring proof of identity, such as a birth certificate. You usually need permission from the parents.

The cash from a grandparent won’t be taxed at the point it was given, though it could be caught by inheritance tax depending on when the gift was made.

Parents and step-parents can become liable for tax on their child’s savings interest, so it’s something to keep an eye on.

If over £100 interest or dividends is earned on the money you give a child, the interest will be taxed as if it were yours. This does not apply to Junior Isas or child trust funds.

This rule only applies to parents – friends and grandparents can give as much as they like without worrying about the interest.

As for the possibility of children paying tax on savings interest, that could also happen – but it’s much less likely. As with adults, children are liable for tax and have a personal allowance of £12,570, plus a £5,000 starting savings allowance and a £1,000 personal savings allowance. If annual income, including interest, exceeds the personal allowance, then income tax will be due.

This depends on the account and the age of the child. Generally, if your child is under eight the account is held in trust by the adult who opened it. Some accounts allow the parent to remain the signatory until your child is 16.

Many accounts let children take control of their savings from the age of seven, meaning they can deposit and withdraw money.

As a parent, you can open a savings account for your child from when they’re born – doing so means you can get saving for them straightaway, so their money can grow and compound over the longest time possible.

Certain accounts will have specific age restrictions – particularly those that offer some form of access for your child. These can be great when your children get a bit older, commonly from around age seven, as a way to teach them about saving and the value of money. Providers will be able to advise what age restrictions are applicable for specific accounts.

data.title: If Scott Mills was the dreary uncle, Sara Cox is like your fun, fag-smoking auntie
data.description:
Radio 2’s relationship with its Breakfast Show hosts in the past few years has been a bit like a Hollywood romcom. The unlucky-in-love radio station has bounced between the warm and mumsy (Zoe Ball) and the dogged but dreary (Scott Mills), only to find that the perfect match was there all along – the faithful, funny best pal who’s always on hand with a glass of wine. So Radio 2 stalwart Sara Cox, after Mills’s precipitous fall from grace, has been given the keys to the nation’s most prestigious radio show. It’s a sensible move from the BBC, which must be wondering why it overlooked Cox for Mills in the first place.

That’s not to say that Cox is a “safe pair of hands” (we said that, derisorily, about Mills when he was appointed). She began her career on Channel 4’s controversial late-night “ladette” programme The Girlie Show, a lewd and loud cavalcade of smut, booze and lads with their tops off. As a presenter on The Big Breakfast and then the Radio 1 Breakfast Show, the earthy Boltonian was a tabloid poster girl for lairy late nights. However, Cox has emerged from the Wild West of the Nineties/Noughties media world as one of the good guys.

Her journey has mirrored that of many Radio 2 listeners (indeed, Cox could be called the apex “Mood Mum”). Since the wild-child years, she’s married, had children, and become an author, as well as hosting BBC Two’s literary show Between the Covers. Last month she raised £11.5m for Children in Need by running 135 miles in five days. However, what gives Cox her edge is that she has never pretended to be squeaky clean. This is, after all, the woman who, on Radio 1, gave us details of how she lost her virginity in a field of sheep. The skeletons in her closet were all out partying till the small hours in the late 1990s. Cox is, in other words, normal.

Having been a stand-in on the Breakfast Show many times, as well as spending years as an exceedingly popular host of Radio 2’s Drive Time slot, we know what we’ll get with Cox in the mornings. Unlike Mills, she won’t overthink it and she won’t be anything other than herself – lively, a little fruity, a bit messy, extremely likeable. Of all the probable candidates for the role, Cox was the one who you would most like to go for a pint with – like your favourite fag-smoking auntie. She is, in many ways, BBC Radio’s Claudia Winkleman – a fizzy, impish presence who always played second fiddle to the more polished presenters but who bided their time and got their reward. From the summer, mornings on Radio 2 will be that bit brighter.

data.title: Gulf takeover of Hollywood accused of ‘gaslighting the American public’
data.description:
Gulf states could be handed majority ownership of CNN and Channel 5 under proposals that have been attacked in Congress as “gaslighting the American public”.

The Hollywood studio Paramount is seeking pre-approval for its partners Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar to increase their 49.5pc stake in Warner Bros once a $111bn (£81bn) takeover is completed.

This could hand increased sway over CNN owner Warner to the trio. They are investing $24bn in Paramount’s bid, accounting for most of the equity in a deal which also depends on $54bn in new debt.

Paramount, which is led by 43-year-old David Ellison and owns Channel 5 in the UK, this week applied to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) for an exemption that could allow overseas investors up to 100pc ownership with 20pc of voting rights.

Sam Liccardo, a Californian Democratic Congressman, lashed out at the plans and the regulator on Friday.

He said: “Congress did not entrust the public airwaves to this agency so that it could auction off America to Riyadh, Abu Dhabi and Doha. This will not stand.”

Under US laws, foreign investors are blocked from owning more than 25pc of a company with a broadcasting licence. However, the FCC can approve higher shareholdings if it deems that doing so would “serve the public interest”.

Mr Liccardo said: “The commission must not allow a legal technicality to launder what is, in substance, a surrender of American media and infrastructure to the hands of foreign authoritarian regimes.”

In the UK, entities controlled by foreign states are prohibited from holding broadcast licences.

Paramount has made the application to the FCC for a waiver despite having claimed that the Gulf state investors will be entirely passive and not hold any governance rights over Warner.

The studio described the application as a “customary petition” and insisted it had no plans to increase foreign investors’ equity holdings or hand them any voting rights.

In addition to backing from Gulf states, Paramount’s bid is being funded by the billionaire Ellison family and RedBird Capital, the second-largest shareholder in Paramount.

The US private equity firm has been involved in two aborted attempts to acquire control of The Telegraph in the past three years alongside the United Arab Emirates. It stumbled on political and regulatory obstacles despite making similar arguments that the Gulf autocracy would play only a passive role.

A Paramount spokesman said: “When the [Warner] transaction and equity syndication close, the Ellison family and RedBird will collectively hold the largest equity stake in the combined company and continue to be the sole owners of Class A Common Stock, representing 100pc of the voting shares, with no other equity syndication party having any governance rights, voting shares, or board representation.

“The combination of Paramount and WBD’s complementary assets will enhance competition while creating a strong champion for creative talent and consumer choice.”

The studio declined to respond to Mr Liccardo’s letter.

Similar exemptions for foreign ownership have previously been granted in deals involving media companies such as Univision and iHeartMedia.

The Warner takeover has nevertheless run into significant opposition over its Gulf ties, particularly in California, where Hollywood unions have been campaigning against it alongside well-known actors including Robert De Niro and Glenn Close.

In a letter to the FCC Mr Liccardo said: “The company [Paramount] disingenuously dismisses this as a ‘procedural maneuver’, but the Commission should not fall for it. The sovereign wealth funds involved represent authoritarian regimes with complex and, at times, adversarial relationships with US foreign policy objectives and norms of press freedom.”

He also highlighted the planned shareholding in the combined company for the Chinese tech company Tencent, “a US designated Chinese military company, with close ties to the Communist Party of China”. RedBird’s Chinese ties also stoked controversy in its bids for The Telegraph.

The takeover of Warner is also under scrutiny on this side of the Atlantic.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) is seeking initial views on the deal ahead of its phase one investigation, while Paramount is expected to file its submission to the European Commission next month.

Ofcom, which issues Channel 5’s broadcasting licence, will also review the impact of the deal on news plurality and carry out a “fit and proper” test on the new ownership.

The Ellison family has provided a backstop for the entire $47bn equity portion of the bid, meaning they would step up to fund the deal if the Gulf backing fell through. David Ellison’s 81-year-old father Larry is the founder of the database giant Oracle and has a net worth estimated by Bloomberg at $213bn.

Larry Ellison’s closeness to Donald Trump was touted by Paramount as offering investors “greater regulatory certainty” compared with a rival bid from Netflix.

Paramount has outlined plans to cut $6bn in costs at Warner, sparking concerns about job losses in an already depressed Hollywood economy. However, the company has insisted the deal will not lead to fewer films being made or released in cinemas.

data.title: AI’s carbon emissions will be up to 136,000pc higher than thought
data.description:
The Government has admitted it underestimated AI’s carbon emissions by as much as 136,000pc.

AI computing is now expected to generate between 34 and 123 megatonnes of carbon dioxide by 2035, according to new figures published by the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

That would be up to 3.4pc of the UK’s total emissions over the next decade.

The new estimates are between 87,000pc and 136,000pc higher than previous figures released last summer, when the Government said emissions from AI would be less than 0.05pc of Britain’s total.

DSIT did not say what had made it revisit the estimates but it said the figures had been updated “to reflect new analysis”.

AI data centres require huge amounts of electricity to operate, much of which is still generated by carbon-emitting fossil fuels.

The Government published a “compute roadmap” last year, laying out how the UK could boost data centre infrastructure and fulfil Labour’s AI goals.

The original report included analysis from the consultancy Cambridge Econometrics to estimate AI’s carbon emissions.

The emissions estimates are not the result of new forecasts for AI’s growth, which remain the same as last year, but they revise how carbon usage is calculated.

“Scenario projections are subject to uncertainty and may evolve as further evidence and perspectives are incorporated,” the report said.

The original analysis claimed that AI’s emissions over a decade would be equivalent to the annual emissions of 23,600 UK households.

However, under the new analysis, it would amount to 20.5 million households – a figure that was not included in the revised report.

The figures will fuel concerns about the technology’s environmental impact, raising questions about whether Sir Keir Starmer’s AI ambitions are compatible with net zero.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has said that how data centres affect net zero targets is “inherently uncertain”.

Climate journal Carbon Brief had found that official estimates for the UK as a whole were smaller than estimates for individual data centres.

The wide range of the official estimates is dependent on how quickly Britain’s electricity grid decarbonises.

“If successful, the UK’s grid decarbonisation plans would help to reduce emissions from data centres towards the bottom end of this range,” DSIT said.

MPs on the environmental audit committee have said they will investigate how data centres affect the climate.

A planned data centre in Buckinghamshire, which would be one of Britain’s biggest if constructed, is expected to rely entirely on gas power because of the long wait for a grid connection.

Ofgem has said that if every planned data centre received a grid connection, Britain’s energy use could double.

AI computing is significantly more power-intensive than typical data centres and has led to a race for electricity connections.

In the US, politicians have raised concerns that households could face higher electricity bills as a result.

The Government was contacted for comment.

data.title: ‘Ozempic breath’ fuelling demand for mints and chewing gum
data.description:
“Ozempic breath” brought on by weight-loss jabs is fuelling a rise in demand for mints and chewing gum.

Hershey, which produces Reese’s and KitKats, said sales of its Ice Breaker brand of mints rose by 8pc in the three months to March.

The company attributed the increase to more widespread use of GLP-1s, which slow down digestion and can cause side effects including nausea, a dry mouth and burping.

Around 1.6 million UK adults used GLP-1 weight-loss drugs in the year to early 2025, according to a University College London study, with estimates suggesting more than two million are now paying privately as uptake surges.

Kirk Tanner, the company’s chief executive, said: “We’ve seen strong demand for gum and mint products as the category benefits from functional snacking tailwinds, including GLP-1 adoption.”

He said the group had “kicked off the year strong” and was on track to hit its financial targets for 2026.

Hershey has come under increased cost pressure from surging cocoa prices, which have forced the company to raise prices.

The conflict in the Middle East has also raised concerns about further spikes as higher fertiliser costs pile pressure on farmers.

‘Consumer macro pressure’

The US-based chocolate maker said consumers were becoming “more deliberate” in their spending amid ongoing economic uncertainty but it maintained that demand for confectionery remained resilient.

It said “consumer macro pressure” continued to weigh on performance, even as core categories held up.

Overall sales at the group rose by 10.6pc as steady demand and price rises offset a dip in sales volumes.

Price increases of about 10pc helped drive growth during the quarter, despite a roughly 2pc fall in volumes.

It added that sales of protein bars had soared by 17pc in the last three months. The rise of weight loss jabs has sent more people seeking protein-dense food to avoid losing muscle.

Hershey is one of many consumer goods businesses to cite weight loss jabs in recent results.

The company behind Ben & Jerry’s reported on Wednesday that consumers were switching to smaller ice creams as a result of weight loss jabs.

The Magnum Ice Cream Company, which was spun out of consumer giant Unilever last year, said sales rose 4.5pc in the three months to March, beating analyst forecasts and investor fears that weight loss jabs would hit sales.

Meanwhile, its former parent company, Unilever, reported that weight loss jabs had unexpectedly reduced demand for its hair loss brand Nutrafol.

The company said online pharmacists often marketed rival hair loss products alongside weight loss jabs, as hair loss is a common side effect of GLP-1s.

data.title: Fracking is back – but not as you know it
data.description:
It was a battle environmentalists thought they had won years ago. But fracking – the controversial technology for extracting oil and gas by cracking underground rocks – is about to stage a comeback.

Not, however, under its old name. The F-word, as some refer to it, is banned.

The companies seeking planning permission and licences to crack open Britain’s oil and gas bearing rocks are now talking about “proppant squeeze”, “reservoir enhancement” or simply “stimulation”.

Whatever it is called, fracking, albeit of a gentler kind, is reappearing.

Rathlin Energy won a licence from the Environment Agency this year to drill and “stimulate” a gas field discovered near Hull.

The West Newton field, thought to hold up to eight billion cubic metres of gas, is expected to connect to the UK gas transmission system within a few years and boost Britain’s energy security.

First the company will have to pump thousands of cubic feet of water into its wells to split the rock apart sufficiently for gas to flow out. While the volumes of fluid and the pressures will all be reduced, critics argue this remains fracking in all but name.

Today, similar schemes are being considered around the UK – partly because data suggest that there is a lot more oil and gas under the UK than realised. Fracking in all its forms offers a way to reach it.

Onshore oil and gas is already big. Research by the industry’s trade body, UK Onshore Oil and Gas (UKOOG), shows the UK has 120 active sites and 250 operating wells, producing 20,000 to 25,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day.

What lies in store could be far greater. “There are estimated potential reserves of 1,329 trillion cubic feet of shale gas under central England,” says UKOOG. “That compares to the UK’s annual consumption of just over three trillion cubic feet.”

In other words, there could be enough gas under England to power the country for centuries.

However, whether it is sensible to target these reserves in the current climate is another question. With Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, planning a legal ban on fracking, and oil and gas companies facing the country’s toughest corporate taxes, the question remains: why push fracking now?

The short answer is money. Fracking has been the single most disruptive force in energy over the last 20 years – reversing global flows of oil and gas and the geopolitical power balances they fuel.

So far America has been the biggest beneficiary.

Before 2000, the US oil industry was in steady decline. The “easy” oil, mainly in porous sandstone, was largely exploited, leaving America increasingly reliant on Middle East imports.

Fracking’s expansion 26 years ago reversed those dynamics – allowing engineers to tap into the vast reserves of oil and gas locked up in America’s shale deposits.

Shale is a dense sedimentary rock that holds its oil and gas in tiny disconnected pores. Breaking that out requires someone to pump a high-pressure mix of water, sand, and chemicals into the rock to literally crack or fracture it. The resulting fissures become a pathway for the fuel to flow out.

Fracking was accompanied by horizontal drilling where engineers worked out how to drill into an oil or gas reserve and then turn the drill-bit sideways.

This meant they could follow an oil or gas-bearing layer of rock for miles, fracking all the way, to drain ever more of its oil and gas.

Such innovations have made the US the world’s greatest oil and gas producer. Last year it produced 13.6m barrels of oil a day – 13pc of global demand. Its gas output was 3.4 billion cubic metres, 20 times the UK’s needs.

That surge has powered the American economy for two decades. Today, Britain imports much of its oil and gas from America, meaning it too is dependent on the success of fracking.

Francis Egan once planned to repeat that economic miracle in the UK. His fracking firm, Cuadrilla, targeted the Bowland Shale stretching under northern England — a layer of gas-charged shale up to a kilometre thick.

Back then, in 2008, politicians were keen. Cuadrilla’s fracking approvals, overseen by Miliband in his first stint as energy secretary, were rubber-stamped, opening up the English countryside to drilling for gas.

“Cuadrilla drilled five shale gas wells in Lancashire,” says Egan. “We successfully fracked the shale in three of those wells and flowed very high‑quality natural gas back to surface.”

He says the British Geological Survey reported that the Bowland Shale could contain 1,300 trillion cubic feet of gas, which would be enough to meet UK demands for decades or even centuries.

“Our analysis suggested a modest national development could have delivered peak production approaching total UK household gas demand within a decade,” Egan says.

But Cuadrilla’s drilling came with a price – a rash of small earthquakes culminating in 2019 with a 2.9 magnitude quake that set thousands of homes quivering. An instant ban followed and fracking seemed to be dead.

Today, in its rebadged form as “stimulation”, the process has been toned down, allowing it to no longer be legally classed as fracking.

Geologists say it is not yet clear on if this watered down version will cause the earth to move. Proppant squeeze still cracks open underground rocks but not so much, says Brian Baptie, a seismologist with the British Geological Survey, with evidence suggesting this avoids tremors.

However, University of Edinburgh geologist and fracking specialist Prof Stuart Haszeldine says the process is little different to the old fracking.

“Proppant squeeze produces exactly the same effect of fracturing the rock for hydrocarbon production as one big injection of high volume fracking, but does it by using smaller volume fracking along the borehole. There clearly are risks of water contamination and of earthquake-triggering.”

Fracking quakes are, however, typically small – similar to those that regularly affected thousands of square miles of the UK when we had an active coal industry.

Some say we would soon get used to them. However, any discussion of fracking – regardless of the name – fires up environmental groups that demand a complete ban on the technology.

Lorraine Inglis, of Frack Free Sussex, says proppant squeeze and reservoir stimulation are “all forms of hydraulic fracturing”, adding: “It’s wordplay designed to mislead. The strategy is obvious: frack using different language and normalise it.

“The risk of earthquakes is real and hasn’t gone away. The UK moratorium exists because seismicity couldn’t be predicted, and that doesn’t change at lower volumes.”

Miliband is nowadays sympathetic to these concerns, having long ago abandoned his former support. He has instead pledged to reinforce the moratorium on high-pressure fracking with a legal ban, although is less definitive on whether stimulation or proppant squeeze are acceptable.

An Department for Energy spokesman said: “We intend to ban fracking for good and make Britain a clean energy superpower, to bring energy security, lower bills and protect current and future generations.”

Such discussions are political as well as technical. The data showing Britain potentially has enough shale gas to meet its needs for centuries will put fracking in all its forms at the heart of debates about energy security for years to come – including May’s local elections and the next general election.

Richard Tice, the energy spokesman for Reform UK, says his party would put such issues at the heart of its manifesto. “It is our patriotic duty to use this valuable vital resource. Leaving it in the ground whilst relying on imported energy is nothing shy of negligence.”

data.title: OVO on cusp of being bought by German rival
data.description:
Ovo Energy, one of Britain’s biggest energy suppliers, is on the cusp of being bought by German rival E.ON as the company races to secure its future.

Plans to combine the two Big Six energy providers would form the biggest household supplier in the country by customer numbers, marking the sector’s biggest deal in decades

Sales talks have been accelerating of late and a deal is said to be possible within weeks, though it is not yet certain, according to Sky News. It is unclear if the Ovo Energy name would disappear from the market.

Ovo Energy, which was founded by billionaire Stephen Fitzpatrick in 2009, became one of Britain’s biggest suppliers in 2020 after it acquired SSE’s retail business for £400m.

But it has been plagued by financial problems, reporting a net loss of £135m in 2024. Last year, it warned that a “material uncertainty” hung over its future because it was having difficulty meeting financial resilience targets set by regulator Ofgem.

It has also racked up £17m in regulatory fines in the last decade, including a £2.7m charge earlier this year for failing to pass on government support payments quickly enough.

Ovo Energy has been struggling for months to source a cash injection or sell its retail business to save its future. Last year, David Buttress, its chief executive, stepped down and the firm axed hundreds of jobs in an effort to reduce costs.

Mr Fitzpatrick is also the owner of luxury private members’ club Kensington Roof Gardens, which posted a £26m loss in December.

A deal with E.ON will dash Mr Fitzpatrick’s hopes of regaining control of Ovo after he failed to convince other shareholders of his plans to inject £200m into the business.

A merger between E.ON and Ovo Energy would create a giant supplier with around 9.7 million customer accounts. This means they would surpass Octopus Energy, which has more than eight million, as Britain’s biggest household supplier.

Ashley Kelty, analyst at Panmure Liberum, said: “It would be the biggest deal in at least a generation. There hasn’t been consolidation on that scale for a long time.”

The two companies combined would become the largest provider of domestic electricity, covering 28pc of the UK market.

However, the company would still only be the third largest provider of domestic gas, covering 23pc of the market, after Octopus and British Gas, which cover 27pc and 25pc respectively.

The deal comes as households are about to get hit by a £280 surge in energy bills as the war in Iran drives up oil and gas prices.

The energy price cap, which is the maximum amount energy suppliers can charge per unit of energy set by regulator Ofgem, is expected to rise by 18pc in July, taking annual energy bills for a typical household from £1,641 to £1,929. Ofgem will announce the next price cap level by May 27.

E.ON and Ovo Energy will likely be able to make significant cost savings from the merger, but customers are unlikely to see this translated into cheaper energy bills because the companies’ profit margins are slim, Mr Kelty said.

E.ON has about 5.7 million customers, while Ovo Energy has four million.

It is not yet clear whether E.ON will face competition in its takeover plans. French utility provider EDF Energy and London-listed Telecom-plus had also previously reportedly expressed interest.

However, it is understood that EDF has effectively ruled itself out of the running and has not held talks with Ovo since earlier this year.

Martin Young, founder of utility policy consultancy Aquaicity, warned that many of these households may be unhappy at the prospect of a new overhaul of their energy supplier and Ovo could lose customers as a result.

Mr Young said: “A decent chunk of Ovo’s customers will have been with SSE and might be apprehensive about going through another integration, given that these aren’t necessarily straightforward.

“There is a possibility of customer loss, particularly at this time of energy market volatility, where the summer price cap could be a trigger point for customer switching.”

E.ON and Ovo Energy declined to comment.

data.title: Miliband brands BP’s profits ‘morally and economically wrong’
data.description:
Ed Miliband has branded BP’s profits “morally and economically wrong” after the war in Iran helped the oil major more than double its earnings.

The Energy Secretary launched an attack on the FTSE 100 giant as the company revealed profits hit $3.2bn (£2.4bn) between January and March, compared with $1.4bn in 2025. BP described the results as “exceptional”.

Mr Miliband condemned the company for making billions of pounds while households face higher energy bills because of rising oil and gas prices.

Posting on X, he wrote: “Profiting from a crisis is morally and economically wrong. That’s why we are taxing these windfall profits to help fund support with the cost of living. And why the Tories, Reform and SNP are utterly wrong to oppose the windfall tax.”

The Energy Secretary later deleted his post and replaced it with a toned-down version. In it, he said instead that it would be “completely wrong for a government to stand by and allow companies to make excess profits from a war”.

His comments triggered a backlash from oil experts.

BP’s surge in profits was driven largely by its oil trading business.

The company is one of the world’s largest oil traders, betting on price swings caused by geopolitical events such as tensions in the Middle East. BP often trades four billion barrels of oil a year – more than double what it actually produces.

Critics claimed this meant that Mr Miliband was wrong to suggest Labour is “taxing these windfall profits” .

The UK windfall tax only applies to oil and gas produced in UK waters, not to global oil trading. Profits made overseas are also taxed in the countries where they are recorded and cannot be taxed again in the UK.

Miliband’s ‘deliberately dishonest narrative’

Russell Borthwick, who runs the chamber of commerce in Aberdeen, accused Mr Miliband of dishonesty.

He said: “Energy companies pay their taxes, where due, on the profits they make. Ed Miliband knows this but he is peddling a deliberately dishonest narrative in relation to the North Sea.”

Mr Miliband’s attack is also likely to revive tensions with Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, who is expected to bank billions of pounds in additional taxes as a result of energy companies’ inflated oil and gas profits.

The row over BP’s profit comes ahead of an expected surge in earnings at all the global oil majors, fuelled by the conflict in the Middle East.

US and Israeli attacks on Iran have led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows.

The closure has caused huge swings in the price of Brent crude.

The oil price hit $112 on Tuesday, its highest level since Donald Trump announced a ceasefire with Iran earlier this month. At the start of the year, it was trading at less than $60 a barrel.

BP’s update on Tuesday marked its first set of results under Meg O’Neill, its new chief executive who started on April 1. She said the company was “running well”.

BP shares have been the sector’s worst-performing since 2020 after a botched net-zero investment drive.

However, the recent oil price jump has reversed that slide, with BP’s shares rising by around 24pc since the start of the war.

BP rose by more than 2pc in early trading on Tuesday, although it later dropped back down.

The company’s strength in oil trading comes from its control of actual oil movements. It manages around 300 chartered and owned tankers, allowing it to reroute cargoes around the world to wherever offers the best price.

Greg Newman, of Onyx Capital, a London-based trader in oil and oil derivatives, said Mr Miliband seemed to misunderstand how the industry worked.

“Oil trading desks exist precisely to manage risk and keep physical barrels moving when markets are dislocated. Without that function, price spikes are sharper, supply disruptions are worse and consumers pay more, not less.

“Risk-takers earn variable returns, that’s the entire basis of the activity.

“As someone who runs an oil trading business in London, I can tell you the last two years have been brutal for large parts of this industry – firms have folded, desks have been shut, books have lost serious money. No one was calling for a rebate then. You don’t get to claim the upside is immoral and ignore the downside that comes with it.”

However, environmental groups were quick to criticise the scale of BP’s profits, claiming they justified the windfall tax imposed on UK offshore operators including BP.

Simon Francis, of End Fuel Poverty, said: “These astronomical profits are a startling reminder that when conflict drives up the price of oil and gas, energy companies profit and households pay.

“We are now seeing the level of profits being posted, which shows why a windfall tax is so necessary.”

Overall profits attributable to shareholders in the first three months of the year soared to $3.8bn, up from $700m in the same period last year.

BP declined to comment on Mr Miliband’s remarks.

data.title: Time has moved on, but our motto is as relevant as ever
data.description:
The Telegraph has a new logo for its newsletter, From the Editor. It is a mash-up of elements from the newspaper’s old clock on Fleet Street and the older emblem of the electric telegraph

From the first, in 1855, The Telegraph relied on new technology. That accounted for the telegraph, a “name we trust appropriate, from the sources of our special information”.

The paper was founded during a war with Russia, and a month before its first issue came out, an electric telegraph cable had been laid from the front in the Crimea, linking all the way back to London, which received news at the speed of lightning before it reached the enemy metropolis of St Petersburg.

It meant that on the second day of its existence, The Daily Telegraph could rush out an extra edition with news of the death of Lord Raglan, the British commander in chief.

On April 19, 1858, an engraved device appeared in the paper that still heads our leading articles: a pictorial celebration of the electric telegraph. Above the Thistle and the Rose, standing for the Union of the kingdom, stretched telegraph wires, familiar from those following every railway line in the land. A pair of wings, for speed, held aloft a stylised telegraph dial.

The electric telegraph worked by sending combinations of two signals, made by rapidly turning a handle to the right or the left. Its binary combination resembled Morse code. The single needle that waved so fast was shown on the dial depicted in the paper, around which appeared the mysterious words: WAS · IS · & · WILL · BE.

These words meant that The Telegraph was rooted in history, devoted to current news and committed to future improvement: “We shall be guided by a high tone of independent action; we shall be bound to the fetters of no party; we will be fearlessly independent,” as it declared in its first issue.

Within 20 years, The Daily Telegraph boasted the “largest circulation in the world”. The paper’s optimism for the future encouraged it to demolish the grand marble-halled building in Fleet Street opened by the Prince of Wales in 1882. After only 46 years, it was replaced with an even grander building in Art Deco style with soaring giant pillars of Portland stone and decorative Egyptian elements.

In the centre of the fourth floor jutted out a stunning clock. There had been a clock there before, with the letters of the word Telegraph standing in for nine hours. Workmen with alarming confidence and no safety harnesses dismantled it in 1930 in favour of a huge clock mounted in a multicoloured, modern casing of blue, red, green and gold that outdid the most ambitious cinema design.

The new Telegraph clock stood for modernity and up-to-the-minute news. Fleet Street, running from St Paul’s Cathedral to the Law Courts, could set its activities by it.

Flanking the great clock, on the highest visible storey, two winged masks were carved into the stone representing “The Past” and “The Future”. They were the work of Samuel Rabin, the sculptor responsible for the Modernist carving of the West Wind on Charles Holden’s stupendous London Underground building at 55 Broadway in 1928. In addition to his artistic achievements, Rabin had just won an Olympic medal in wrestling.

One more element distinguished the Telegraph building in Fleet Street: in a carving above the door, a pair of naked messengers, each with a caduceus staff, a winged helmet and winged heels, straining to take the news from London to the world.

After 57 years in this ark of Modernism, beneath which printing presses hummed into the night, The Telegraph moved to the Isle of Dogs. In 1994, as the first British daily on the internet, it began to show that papers did not live by paper alone, but by great good fortune it continued to support its IS with its WAS and its WILL BE.

Christopher Howse is the author of How We Saw It: 150 years of The Daily Telegraph.
