data.title: Best easy-access accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
In their purest form, easy-access accounts provide straightforward access to your cash. They can be the simplest type of savings account to hold, and are a good idea if you want to regularly deposit and withdraw funds.

Their convenience can mean lower interest rates, which can increase or decrease at any time (though your provider should always let you know in advance).

They can also come with tricky terms that you should check before you sign up, such as bonus periods and withdrawal restrictions.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains:

  • What is an easy-access savings account?
  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best easy-access savings accounts for May 2026
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account
  • Variable interest vs fixed-rate
  • Easy access account FAQs

What is an easy-access savings account?

Easy-access accounts usually have variable interest rates, which can be increased and decreased at the provider’s discretion – but it should always let you know before the rate changes.

However, these accounts can come with a range of terms that make them more complicated. For instance, some include introductory bonus rates, which are essentially temporary offers to attract new customers. They’re usually good deals when you first sign up, but you’ll need to keep an eye on when they end and might want to switch as soon as they do.

Some accounts also have limited access – for instance, only allowing a certain number of withdrawals each year. If you exceed these, you may be punished with a lower interest rate or the account may automatically close. To avoid any nasty surprises, it’s worth checking the terms to make sure you’re clear on how the account works before you sign up.

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, such as those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS), which covers up to £120,000 per person, per financial institution. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

Best easy-access savings accounts for May 2026

See the table below to see the current top-rate easy-access savings accounts.

There are lots of other accounts that will let you access your cash whenever you want to. See our guides to the best current accounts that pay interest, the best regular accounts and best notice accounts – not to mention our guide to the best cash Isas, which lists out a range of the top tax-free accounts.

For options on the top rates when you lock your cash away for a year or more, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Easy-access accounts are useful for keeping an emergency fund to cover any unexpected costs, as they allow savers to dip in and out when needed.”

But there are downsides, she added: “As the money isn’t locked away, it may be more tempting for savers to unnecessarily withdraw cash.

“The interest rates on easy-access accounts are variable, so could drop at short notice. In some cases, the number of withdrawals may be limited and, if exceeded, may cause the rate to drop dramatically.”

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, added: “Easy-access accounts are probably the most popular in the market at the moment, offering good rates.

“However, clients do need to be aware that this rate is not guaranteed. It is important to monitor the easy-access product as quite often clients forget and then a year later, realise the rate is not attractive.”

Variable interest vs fixed-rate

Easy access accounts are almost always variable, which means the interest rate can change at any time. This tends to follow changes to the Bank of England’s Bank Rate, which is why savings rates are currently on the decline.

Fixed-rate accounts, as the name suggests, keep the same rate for the duration of the fixed term.

Both have pros and cons, depending on what’s happening with the savings market. Signing up to a fixed rate can shield you in times where rates are on the way down and, to compensate for the fact that most of these accounts don’t allow withdrawals during the fixed term, the rates they offer are usually higher.

However, in times when savings rates are rising, having your cash stuck in a fixed rate account can mean you’ll miss out on higher interest elsewhere – so a variable-rate account could be the better option.

Easy-access account FAQs

While instant-access and easy-access accounts are very similar, the slight difference is that instant-access accounts allow you to make withdrawals directly from the account, with no limit on how much you can withdraw or when you can take money out.

An easy-access account can include some restrictions, such as those we’ve mentioned above. Easy-access accounts might also have daily withdrawal limits and you might need to transfer the cash to a current account before you can actually spend it.

It’s generally accepted that you should have between three and six months’ worth of living expenses saved to fall back on in an emergency. If you’re retired, this increases to one to three years’ worth. It’s good to hold this money in an easy-access account that allows you to withdraw quickly, whenever you might need it.

If you have larger sums that you don’t need access to, you might find a better rate by putting it in a fixed-rate bond, or you might consider investing.

The minimum balance for an easy-access account can vary hugely, and it’s not uncommon to see accounts requiring anything from as little as £1 up to £10,000. Shop around for the account which best suits your needs and budget.

data.title: Canary Wharf sounds alarm over Labour’s rent reforms
data.description:
The owner of Canary Wharf has sounded the alarm over Labour’s rent reforms as landlords brace for new red tape over letting out homes.

Canary Wharf Group (CWG), which owns and manages most of the Docklands area of east London, pointed to the threat from the Renters’ Rights Act, which comes into effect on May 1.

It said the new regulation – which is designed to make renting more secure by banning Section 21 “no-fault” evictions – risked denting its business by heaping more costs and administrative burdens onto its estate.

Under the changes, landlords will only be able to increase rents once a year and will not be able to accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals. The Renters’ Rights Act will also mean landlords cannot accept more than one month’s rent in advance.

The regulations have been in the pipeline for some time. A bill proposing to overhaul the private rented housing sector was first introduced to the Commons by Michael Gove, then the housing secretary, in 2023. However, it did not pass before that parliament dissolved for the general election. Labour later introduced a new version of the bill.

CWG, which is owned by Brookfield and the Qatar Investment Authority, raised concerns about “new and emerging regulatory requirements” which included the Renters’ Rights Act.

In its annual report, the Canary Wharf owner said the regulations would create “increased operational complexity and risk of non-compliance”.

CWG also pointed to the growing threat from Labour’s tax raids.

It said it was concerned over “Government-led changes to tax legislation directly impacting the group’s cost base as well as those of our tenants which may contribute to insolvency and credit risk among tenant groups”.

CWG said it expected “ongoing uncertainty ... at both the local and national government levels, increasing the risk of further volatility and uncertainty”.

The warning comes as the east London financial district battles to turn around its fortunes, having struggled with rising interest rates and wavering office demand in recent years.

More recently, it has seen growing demand for office sites. Major companies planning to set up shop in the financial district include Visa and British bank Zopa, while HSBC and Spanish lender BBVA have also signed for office space.

However, as the owner of five rental housing blocks worth £209m, managed by a subsidiary, CWG said it was exposed to a “persistent” squeeze on renters caused by the cost-of-living crisis and a lack of homes being built, driving up rents.

It said it was seeking to mitigate the potential impact by offering more options for people seeking short-to-medium stays.

The company posted an underlying pre-tax loss of £8.2m in 2025 – falling from a profit of £12m in the previous year.

A Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government spokesman said: “The Renters’ Rights Act gives good landlords the confidence they need to continue to invest in the private rented sector.

“The estimated net core cost to landlords is £12 per rented property annually – approximately 0.1pc of mean annual rents.”

data.title: Labour plans to blanket lakes with floating solar farms
data.description:
Ed Miliband is preparing to blanket reservoirs and lakes with solar farms as part of Labour’s push towards net zero.

The Energy Secretary will launch a consultation to make it easier to build floating solar power plants, after a report that hailed their potential as a clean energy source.

Floating solar schemes use the same panels as land-based projects but are mounted on platforms floated in freshwater bodies such as reservoirs, lakes, quarry lakes and industrial ponds.

According to new research commissioned by energy investor Bluefield, there is potential to build up to 58.6 gigawatts of capacity by 2050, enough to power millions of homes at peak sunny conditions.

Under the most ambitious scenario, the solar farms would cover approximately 33,500 hectares – about five times the area of Loch Lomond, Scotland’s largest freshwater lake.

The CBI Economics report said the floating plants would be mostly built on reservoirs operated by water companies and other privately owned man-made lakes.

But it also predicts that up to 2.5pc or 7,375 hectares of the country’s natural lakes could be covered – about five times the size of Windermere in the Lake District, Cumbria.

The proposals were endorsed by Michael Shanks, the energy minister, who said the floating solar farms would boost British energy security.

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero confirmed that schemes of 100 megawatts or more would qualify for “nationally significant” status under changes introduced by Labour, meaning ministers could overrule councils to approve them.

Separately, the Government is preparing to consult on further changes to “permitted development” rights that would make it easier for companies to build without planning permission.

Controversial plans

Experts and campaigners cautiously welcomed the plans but warned that protected natural landscapes such as the Lake District should be excluded.

It is understood that, under the current proposals, national parks would be considered inappropriate locations for floating solar farms.

However, the policy could still prove contentious in some rural communities where there is already a backlash against several large onshore solar and wind farms being built under Labour’s net zero rollout.

Richard Tice, the energy spokesman for Reform UK, described the plans as “another hare-brained folly proposed by the increasingly desperate Miliband”.

He said: “We don’t need any of this extra cost and risk. Nuclear and gas will provide the electricity we need for many decades to come at the lowest cost.”

Jackie Copley, of the Campaign to Protect Rural England, said floating solar panels could be “a smart and creative opportunity that could harness solar energy without putting more pressure on the countryside”.

But she added: “Protected landscapes should not be put in jeopardy, however, and more information is required about the potential environmental impact.

“The Government’s focus should be rooftop solar, which harnesses underused existing structures and has the potential to generate 60pc of the UK’s total solar energy without putting further pressure on the countryside.”

In the report for Bluefield, published on Wednesday, CBI Economics experts modelled three scenarios for floating solar deployment: limited, central or ambitious.

Under the “ambitious” scenario, 9.7 square miles of lakes owned by the water industry would be covered in panels by 2030, along with 1,950 hectares of other man-made lakes and about 738 hectares of natural lakes.

But by 2050, this would rise to 29 square miles in the water industry, 72 square miles for man-made lakes and 28 square miles for natural lakes.

For comparison, Windermere has a surface area of about 5.7 square miles.

James Armstrong, of Bluefield, said: “Floating solar represents one of the most practical and immediate opportunities to strengthen the UK’s energy security.

“As electricity demand rises sharply – including from new AI and data centre infrastructure – this technology can deliver clean power close to where it’s needed most.”

In a statement backing the report, Mr Shanks added: “It’s time Britain stopped letting our solar potential float on by. For too long, we have failed to harness the huge potential of our reservoirs for floating solar.

“As this report shows, floating solar could generate the equivalent of around 11 gas power stations by 2040 – cutting our dependence on volatile global gas markets we do not control.

“In the wake of the conflict with Iran, which has once again exposed the risks of relying on fossil fuels, we are moving further and faster to deliver clean power – protecting bills and making us energy secure.”

Dillon Smith, of the Conservative Environment Network, also backed the findings, which he said showed that solar deployment was “not in conflict with nature”.

He added that ministers should slash red tape that was holding back investment and expand permitted development rights for floating solar on docks, small on-farm reservoirs and disused quarries.

A spokesman for the Government said all projects would be “subject to rigorous planning processes, and the views of the local community must be taken into account”.

data.title: Green energy tycoons at war over heat pumps
data.description:
Dale Vince has criticised Octopus Energy over its support for heat pumps and the government advisory roles held by its boss.

Mr Vince, a green energy tycoon and Labour donor who founded Ecotricity, said heat pumps were a “niche technology that can’t move the dial” for Britain’s net zero targets and were being “mis-sold to the public”.

He criticised a decision by ministers this year to earmark £2.7bn for subsidies that give households a £7,500 grant to help pay for a pump.

Mr Vince said there were “urgent questions” over whether Greg Jackson, Octopus Energy’s chief executive, had influenced the policy.

He added: “Greg Jackson has nearly as many government roles as an Octopus has tentacles.

“There are significant potential conflicts of interest here, not least the vast sums of public money being spent on heat pump subsidies, while Jackson boasts of being Britain’s biggest seller of heat pumps.

“Octopus stands to gain significantly from the recently announced government policy. The £3bn heat pump subsidy is an exceptional sum of money for a niche technology that can’t move the dial on any of our targets or help in any way with energy poverty.”

Mr Jackson currently holds three unpaid advisory roles in Government. There is no suggestion of wrongdoing by him or Octopus, and a government spokesman said he had not been involved in any discussions about heat pumps.

‘Wild and categorically unfounded’

On Friday, Octopus said the “wild and categorically unfounded insinuations” were “the latest in a string of unprovoked attacks by Mr Vince”.

An Octopus Energy spokesman said: “We are bemused by these wild and categorically unfounded insinuations, the latest in a string of unprovoked attacks by Mr Vince.

“Greg was asked by both this and the previous government to help with his experience in technology, entrepreneurship and growth, which he has done voluntarily.

“Mr Vince would do well to use his undoubted ability and influence to sort his own businesses and to make energy cheaper and more secure for the people of Britain, rather than obsessing over petty rivalry and jealousy.”

Last year, Mr Vince criticised his rival for his “evangelical” support for controversial plans to reform the electricity market and published his own report saying the policy would be a grave error.

“Part of me thinks that he’s looking for a cause to campaign for and make a name for himself,” Mr Vince said at the time.

But Mr Jackson recently insisted that claims about his influence were wide of the mark, pointing out that Octopus had “spent the year being absolutely brutalised” by various government decisions.

Last year, his company unsuccessfully campaigned for regional electricity pricing. In March, it was reported that its attempt to buy low-cost wind turbines from Chinese manufacturer Ming Yang was blocked owing to security concerns.

Octopus said Mr Jackson had declared his conflicts to the Government, recused himself from discussions about energy and “carries out his public service roles with the highest ethical standards”.

Mr Jackson co-founded Octopus in 2015 and has grown the company into Britain’s biggest energy supplier, serving eight million households and selling heat pumps, solar panels, batteries and electric cars.

He is a member of the Government’s industrial strategy advisory council, a non-executive director at the Cabinet Office and co-chairs the CustomerFirst unit in the technology department.

Mr Vince’s Ecotricity, founded in 1995, has around 200,000 customers.

The tycoon has donated more than £5m to the Labour Party but has also backed protest groups including Just Stop Oil and Extinction Rebellion.

He has recently emerged as a vocal opponent of heat pumps and has backed biogas as an eco-friendly alternative.

data.title: Iran war oil shock risks food shortages, warns World Bank
data.description:
The Iran war risks causing food shortages as soaring fertiliser prices push more farmers to the brink, the World Bank has warned.

As many as 45 million people worldwide could face “acute food insecurity” this year if the conflict in the Middle East continues, the organisation said.

It follows a warning that farmers will struggle to keep up food production as they face a 31pc increase in fertiliser prices this year.

About half the world’s supply of urea, a nitrogen-based fertiliser, and almost a third of ammonia typically flow through the Strait of Hormuz.

The World Bank said price rises would be more severe for urea as shipments have ground to a halt since Iran and the US blocked the strait.

The price of urea jumped 50pc in March alone, the biggest monthly increase in a decade. They are expected to rise by 60pc this year.

The World Bank said: “Fertiliser affordability will fall to its worst level since 2022, eroding farmers’ incomes and threatening future crop yields.

“If the conflict proves more prolonged, these pressures on food supply and affordability could push up to 45 million more people into acute food insecurity this year,” the organisation said in its latest commodity report.

Farmers are also grappling with higher diesel and gas prices, making it more expensive to run their machinery. Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens by the end of May, energy prices will still surge 24pc this year to a four-year high, the World Bank said.

Indermit Gill, its chief economist, said: “The war is hitting the global economy in cumulative waves. First, through higher energy prices, then, higher food prices and finally, higher inflation, which will push up interest rates.”

The World Bank had previously forecast that the price of agricultural commodities to fall this year, because the industry began 2026 with a grain surplus. However, the Bank’s food price index is now forecast to increase by 2pc.

Beef prices will surge 11pc this year, the World Bank warned, while palm oil and soybean oil prices will jump by more than 8pc. It said it expected barley and wheat to rise by about 4pc.

If urea prices stay higher for longer, then farmers will cut back on using fertiliser. “These effects could have a long tail, materialising as reduced crop yields in future growing seasons,” the World Bank said.

Oil, gas and fertiliser prices have risen more quickly in the first month of the Iran war than they did in the first month of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The World Bank’s 2026 forecasts are based on disruption in the Strait of Hormuz starting to ease in May, with shipping traffic returning close to pre-war levels by October.

It said: “Commodity prices could rise even higher, if hostilities escalate or supply disruptions from the war last longer than projected.”

data.title: Reeves hands Rolls-Royce £600m to build mini-nukes in Britain
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has been handed a £600m loan from Rachel Reeves to help accelerate the development of Britain’s first mini-nuclear power plant.

The Chancellor’s National Wealth Fund confirmed details of the borrowing facility on Monday, which will be used to build three small modular reactors (SMRs) at Wylfa on the Welsh island of Anglesey.

The manufacturing giant was selected as the Government’s preferred partner for the project last year.

However, design work will now begin after Rolls-Royce struck an agreement with Great British Energy – Nuclear (GBEN), the quango set up to manage the mini-nuclear programme.

GBEN said the contract would unlock the next stage of the project, which aims to help commercialise nascent SMR technology.

It will provide Rolls-Royce with funds to complete detailed design work, including regulatory applications and planning approvals.

The £600m loan is in addition to £2.6bn that Ms Reeves allocated to the scheme during last year’s spending review.

Construction of the power plant itself is not expected to begin until the early 2030s, with the facility set to come online in the mid-2030s. But GBEN is understood to be carrying out ground preparation works now.

The scheme is eventually expected to create 3,000 local jobs in Wylfa, Anglesey, as well as another 5,000 supply chain jobs across the country.

Ms Reeves said: “This investment, along with vital financing from the National Wealth Fund, will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, added: “At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain’s energy security.

“We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, home-grown power for decades to come.”

GBEN said the contract with Rolls-Royce stipulates that the company must hit various “milestones”, although it did not immediately clarify what those were.

The three mini reactors are expected to have a combined capacity of about 1.4 gigawatts, enough to power three million homes for more than 60 years.

SMRs will be constructed from “modules” built in factories and then transported to sites for assembly.

The idea is meant to ensure the plants can be produced efficiently at scale and more quickly than larger nuclear projects.

In the longer run, it is hoped their lower price tags will also make them more attractive to private investors, who have long viewed bigger plants as too expensive and risky to back without government support.

Major technology companies, including Google, Amazon and Facebook-owner Meta, are investing in potential SMR technologies in the hope they could eventually be used to supply power-hungry data centres needed to develop AI.

Rolls-Royce is also building six SMRs for the Czech government.

Simon Bowen, the chairman of GBEN, said: “This is an immense moment for the UK nuclear programme, our organisation and the industry as a whole.

“Today represents a true recognition of the efforts made across GBEN, Rolls-Royce SMR and Government to get to this point, and I’m hugely proud of the team for reaching this critical milestone.”

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, added: “This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme.”

data.title: JCB threatens to quit Britain over inheritance tax raid
data.description:
The heir to JCB’s multibillion-pound manufacturing fortune has threatened to quit Britain in protest against Rachel Reeves’s inheritance tax raid.

Jo Bamford, son of Lord Bamford, the JCB chairman, warned the Staffordshire-based business could easily relocate to America to avoid paying 20pc tax on assets worth more than £2.5m.

“The family tax is a real problem,” Mr Bamford told City AM, warning that JCB “could quite easily become an American business”.

“I love being in Britain,” he said. “I love being here. I love our factories. But I would say to a political party of any stripe, there’s only so much you can ultimately do.”

Founded in 1945, JCB is one of Britain’s biggest family-owned manufacturing companies. It has 11 factories in the UK and employs more than 8,000 workers.

However, Mr Bamford said JCB could consider its future in the UK because Labour is “hunting down” family businesses through its inheritance tax raid.

He said: “You want us, as a family, to invest here in Britain.

“You know, we have businesses everywhere around the world. We have them in India and China and Brazil.”

Mr Bamford added: “I’m here because I’m British, and I’m here and I employ people in Britain because I like British people and I like being in my part of the community.”

The levy was first announced by the Chancellor in late 2024, removing family business owners’ ability to pass down assets through generations without having to pay inheritance tax.

As of April, full tax relief will only apply to the first £2.5m of a business’s assets upon the owner’s death, with everything above that threshold subject to a 20pc tax.

This threshold had initially been set at £1m, but was increased after a backlash from rural voters.

Many business owners are concerned that they will be forced to sell off assets or reduce investment to pay the tax.

This has prompted a string of high-profile entrepreneurs to consider leaving Britain as part of a broader wealth exodus, driven also by Ms Reeves’s decision to abolish non-dom status.

Some have already left since Labour came to power, including Alan Howard, a billionaire hedge fund manager, John Fredriksen, a shipping magnate, and Lakshmi Mittal, a steel tycoon.

Speaking to The Telegraph last year, Sir Rocco Forte, the hotelier, said the inheritance tax changes were “one of the main causes for the exodus”.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We’ve listened and raised the relief threshold to £2.5m to protect more small family businesses, while ensuring the largest make a fair contribution so we can deliver support for families and businesses, including cutting the cost of living.”

data.title: Britain faces food shortages because of Iran war
data.description:
Britain is facing a summer of food shortages because of the Iran war. A shortage of carbon dioxide could mean chicken, pork and fizzy drinks are in short supply if the conflict continues, secret government contingency planning for a “worst-case scenario” reveals.

Senior officials from departments including No 10, the Treasury, and the Ministry of Defence rehearsed scenarios examining the potential impact of the war on British industry in an event codenamed Exercise Turnstone.

The contingency plans, seen by The Times, were based on a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and no deal to end the war has been reached by mid-June. Farmers were warned of potential disruption and hospitality businesses, already squeezed by Labour’s tax rises, could be hit first by a shortage of CO2.

This is because CO2 is used in the process of slaughtering pigs and chickens and making drinks fizzy. It also improves the shelf life of foods such as salads, packaged meats and cakes. 

Officials also believe a collapse in CO2 supplies could endanger lives by making dry ice used to cool blood supplies, organs and vaccines scarcer. Under a reasonable worst-case scenario, CO2 supplies would fall to just 18 per cent of current levels, and factories would be ordered to stop other manufacturing to boost supplies by up to 100 per cent.

Emergency legislation to compel factories to co-operate has been discussed, with suppliers being compensated for stopping production of their main products.

The worst-case scenario involved an important UK plant suffering a mechanical error, while high gas costs led to lower production of ammonia and fertiliser, which produces CO2 as a by-product across Europe.

Critical food shortages are not expected, but shoppers could see fewer variety of goods on supermarket shelves.

Britain is expected to be hit hard by the economic fallout from the war.

On Wednesday, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) forecast that Britain is expected to suffer the biggest hit to growth of any G7 economy.

In a triple blow for Rachel Reeves, the IMF cut its UK growth forecasts for this year and next, warned of the highest inflation in the G7 and predicted one of the smallest increases in living standards in the world.

Higher fuel prices and energy bills are forecast to cost households £480 this year, according to the Resolution Foundation, with middle-class families being hit hardest.

On Thursday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for food shortages was under way and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course we don’t comment on leaks. But this now is out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and that he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

A government spokesman said: “We took decisive action last month to shore up the UK’s critical supplies of CO2 by temporarily restarting the Ensus bioethanol plant in Teesside and are continuing to work closely with business groups to tackle the impacts of events in the Middle East.

“Reasonable worst-case scenarios are a planning tool used by experts and are not a prediction of future events.”

data.title: Britain has record taxes yet Labour’s mismanagement has left us broke
data.description:
The Governor of the Bank of England has warned that the world is facing a “very big energy shock” that will push up prices.

A statement of the bleeding obvious, as Basil Fawlty might say? Perhaps, but it serves to highlight just how insufficient the response has been to the economic fallout from Donald Trump’s misadventures in the Middle East.

The Government is so far out of its depth that armbands and a rubber ring may have to become compulsory items at future Cabinet meetings.

The latest iteration of the energy support scheme for industry is merely further evidence that ministers either still don’t get it or aren’t willing to provide meaningful support.

The decision to rethink the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which provides a 25pc cut in bills for so-called energy-intensive users, was supposed to demonstrate that someone in Government finally had grasped the severity of the situation facing manufacturers.

Instead, the opposite is true. Sure, expanding the breadth of it to include over 10,000 factories rather than the 7,000 first proposed is meaningful, but not if support isn’t brought forward. For reasons no one in power has been able to provide a compelling explanation for, those that qualify for relief still won’t be able to access it until April 2027.

The mind truly boggles. Without real help now, Britain risks losing some of the most critical parts of the supply chain forever, undermining our already fragile national sovereignty further and making us even more reliant on volatile foreign suppliers for materials that underpin scores of vital products.

The American owner of the UK’s last producer of a critical chemical called aniline has warned that it would close the site in Wilton, Teesside, if energy prices remained at their current levels for the next three months.

It means the amended BICS scheme, which Peter Kyle wasted little time in championing on national radio on Thursday, won’t make the slightest jot of difference to the fate of at least one major factory. The future of many more will be hanging in the balance.

The Business Secretary had the audacity to claim the changes to BICS showed Labour was “a government of action” that would “always do what’s needed” in times of global instability. Twaddle.

The only sensible conclusion is that Labour’s woeful mismanagement of the economy, under which welfare is being repeatedly prioritised over everything else including even our national security, has rendered Britain so broke that it could not afford to respond in times of genuine crisis.

This, of course, shouldn’t be the case. As the International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out this week, Rachel Reeves has raised taxes at the fastest pace in the world.

The Chancellor’s repeated raids on households and businesses would push the UK’s tax burden to a peacetime high of 42pc of GDP by the start of the next decade, the IMF’s figures showed. This equates to an extra £130bn of taxes since Labour took power, or a staggering £4,500 more per household.

But because of this Government’s obsession with welfare and the NHS, there is no money for energy or defence – or much else, for that matter. As Anna Leach, the chief economist at the Institute of Directors, points out, the UK is “uniquely vulnerable” to events in the Middle East because of “financing conditions, high initial starting point for inflation, uncompetitive energy costs and low fiscal space”.

It explains why the IMF expects growth in Britain this year to be slower, and inflation higher than any other major advanced economy.

The problem is spending, or rather where that spending is being directed. The intervention of Lord Robertson – a Labour grandee, former secretary-general of Nato, as well as the author of the Government’s recent strategic defence review – is telling.

Perhaps it will prove to be decisive in ensuring that some of the ever-expanding welfare budget is redirected to other departments. When serious figures like Robertson are starting to openly question the benefits bill, it is surely time for a complete rethink of where resources are being channelled. As he points out, Britain’s welfare budget is now five times the amount we spend on defence at a time when national security has never been more vital.

Perhaps naive ministers have allowed the latest GDP figures to fool them into thinking the economy could weather the incoming storm.

No doubt February’s numbers showing a 0.5pc rise in GDP are a pleasant surprise, but any growth had almost certainly already been extinguished by events in the Middle East – Kyle himself acknowledged this.

It is why the failure to provide meaningful and immediate support for heavy industry makes no sense. Left to fend for themselves in the face of even higher energy and raw material costs than they were already grappling with thanks to the war in Ukraine, and endless government-imposed green levies, manufacturers will be forced to curtail investment, cut back on hiring, and possibly even reduce output. Under those circumstances, growth becomes little more than an aspiration.

Yet, at the same time, ministers are drawing up plans to invoke emergency legislation to force many of those very same factories to double CO2 production to avoid shortages that will ripple through the economy. Forget a fizzy drinks crisis, a CO2 scarcity could quickly become a threat to life by affecting supplies of vaccines, blood and vital medicines.

Given this, you would think ministers would be bending over backwards to provide help. Instead, the best they could come up with was a rehashed scheme that still wouldn’t be available for another year – proof that the disconnect at the top has reached epic new proportions.

On Thursday, Sir Keir Starmer told tech bosses hauled into No 10 to discuss child safety: “The status quo simply cannot be allowed to stand.” With local elections around the corner, the same could be said of Labour’s time in power.

data.title: British potteries are in trouble. Here are the everyday ceramics to buy and use
data.description:
News of Denby Pottery calling in the administrators has brought the challenges faced by the British ceramics industry into sharp focus. Rising costs surrounding employment and energy have left the 217-year-old business, which produces all of its collections from its site in Derbyshire, operating at a loss. It is looking for a new buyer and has reportedly cut its 500-plus workforce by 80.

Denby isn’t the only one feeling the pinch. Mike Raybould, the chief executive of Portmeirion Group, says: “Energy remains one of the most significant pressures on our industry. Ceramics production is inherently energy-intensive, so sustained increases in energy prices have a direct impact on the cost of manufacturing in the UK.” Portmeirion, which was founded in the late 1960s, produces 100,000 kiln-fired pieces a week from its factory in Stoke-on-Trent and is stocked in around 60 countries. “The ‘made in the UK’ backstamp carries a huge amount of value in the global market,” adds Raybould.

Public support for Denby has been mobilised with a #SaveDenby campaign, but it will take more than just a hashtag to save the business. Raybould notes that the ceramics sector has called on the Government to be eligible for energy support schemes such as the British Industry Supercharger, which benefits steel, metal and chemical producers.

Then there is the issue around skills and recruitment. Emma Bridgewater, which also produces its ranges in The Potteries, has an ageing workforce. “So many of our highly skilled makers are coming up for retirement. The industry hasn’t had an entry programme or apprenticeship programme since the 1980s,” says a spokesperson. That will change next month, when six new recruits join the inaugural Craft Apprenticeship Scheme, which covers every element of the ceramic manufacturing process.

Emma Bridgewater, which was founded in 1986, has amassed a 100,000-strong base of ardent fans. “All of our pottery is hand-signed, and some customers will collect a particular decorator’s work and come to meet them at the factory, so there’s a real connection,” adds the spokesman. “By investing in buying British pottery to eat your breakfast off, you’re supporting an industry-wide regeneration of skills and expertise in giving young people in a city an opportunity to develop a career. It really reaches deep.”

On that note, here are some of the best buys to elevate the everyday in the kitchen, all proudly made in the UK.

data.title: How to buy and rent out property in Spain
data.description:
This is the first in a series of guides on buying and letting property abroad.

Are you the landlord of an overseas property? We’d like to hear about your experience – email money@telegraph.co.uk

Spain has long been a favourite for British holidaymakers. In 2025, 19.1 million of us flocked to the country to enjoy its great weather, beaches, culture and food.

It’s not surprising, then, that it’s also a popular choice for British expats – around 350,000 are registered as residents in the country – as well as those looking to buy and let holiday homes abroad.

If you’re considering an investment in a Spanish property, there’s a lot to consider first – not least the threat of increased taxes for buyers outside the EU.

Here, Telegraph Money asks mortgage, tax and legal experts to share their tips. This guide will cover:

  • How to find the best investments
  • What yields can I expect?
  • How easy is it to get a mortgage?
  • What are the tax implications?
  • What pitfalls should you be aware of?

How to find the best investments

Choosing a location for your property search in Spain will be dictated by your budget and what you want from your investment. If you plan to holiday there as well or keep it for retirement, it’s important to find a place you love. However, if it’s purely an investment, it comes down to what your ideal tenants will be looking for.

Alex Radford, partner at Spain-based law firm English Solicitor and Abogado, recommends what he calls “lock and goes”. These can be described as simple properties, such as two-bedroom and two-bathroom accommodations, which require minimal maintenance and are in easily accessible areas.

“You want somewhere within an hour of an airport, with good facilities, a swimming pool and access to the beach. You want to walk out and have everything on the doorstep.”

While there might be a temptation to invest in a pretty rural property, you might find it more difficult to find tenants for it. He added: “If you prefer a rural property and want to rent it out, you’ll need a good agent.”

In terms of areas to consider, he said: “The Costa Del Sol is still the hottest spot for property, but we’ve just opened up an office in Mojácar in Almeria.” The area, which has long been a popular spot with Spanish holidaymakers, can be more affordable than other parts of Spain and is becoming increasingly popular with British visitors.

In the Costa del Sol, Radford says you’ll generally need £400,000-£500,000 for a lock and go, but it’s possible to bag bargains starting from £100,000 in the Costa Blanca or Almeria.

What yields can I expect?

The average rental yield in Spain is currently 5.45pc, before tax, according to the Global Property Guide. However, while short-term lets will typically offer higher returns, it’s important to be aware that there will always be significant variations depending on where and what you purchase.

Peter Esders, legal director at Judicare, said: “Traditionally, you would be looking at something like 5-8pc yields, but these will very much depend on not only the region and area but also the property. If you buy the wrong property, then even being in a good area won’t help. Equally, you can buy the best property in the wrong area and won’t be able to rent it out.”

For this reason, it’s a good idea to seek the help of agents with a good knowledge of the area you’re looking to buy in – or to make sure you know the area well yourself.

In addition to a steady income, there is also potential for capital growth, with house prices continuing to rise across Spain. According to figures from Global Property Guide, house prices across Spain were up by 12.89pc in the final quarter of 2025, compared to the same period in the previous year.

However, while the Spanish market might look healthy, there are headwinds for UK investors.

Amid concerns that local buyers cannot afford property in many parts of Spain, the government proposed a new 100pc tax on purchases from buyers outside of the EU last year. This hasn’t yet come to pass – and some say it never will – but locals’ displeasure with foreign buyers hasn’t gone away. 

Earlier this year there were reports the Balearic Islands government was considering banning British buyers from purchasing property in Mallorca, Menorca and Ibiza.

There are plenty of existing rules to abide by. Introduced last year, those entering the short-term rental market must register rental properties with the authorities and get a reference number – you won’t be able to list on booking websites like Airbnb without one.

If you’re buying an apartment in a block, you’ll also need the approval of 60pc of the community of owners to get a rental licence. This is part of moves to give local property owners a say in whether or not they’re happy having holidaymakers as neighbours.

“Some won’t allow it,” said Mr Radford. “Or they might charge more in community fees.”

Complicating matters further is the fact that different regions and authorities within Spain are also able to impose their own rules on the issuing of licences. Seville, for example, has introduced a cap on licences, while Malaga has frozen short-term licences in some districts for three years.

As such, Mr Radford suggests that if you’re looking for an apartment or townhouse in a block, ask your agent to only show you properties that can be rented out.

Before you choose a property, it’s also important to factor in the full cost of buying it, with taxes varying between regions. Mr Radford suggested budgeting for up to 14pc of the purchase price to cover costs, including transfer tax and legal fees.

How easy is it to get a mortgage?

Getting a mortgage overseas isn’t always easy. However, Miranda John, director of international property at mortgage broker SPF Private Clients, said: “The high street Spanish lenders are pretty flexible on where a potential borrower is resident. Spanish mortgages are always on a repayment basis, and LTVs [loan-to-values] are a maximum of 70pc unless you are a Spanish resident.

“For property of €2m or more, this usually falls to 60pc. The pricing is competitive, and there are attractive short and long-term fixed rates.”

She added that if you’re looking to buy a rural property, you might find you need to stump up a bigger deposit, with maximum LTVs potentially limited to 50pc.

Another option is to remortgage your UK property and use the money raised to buy the property outright. Louis Levine, senior mortgage broker at Orton Financial, said: “UK high street lenders are generally comfortable with this, provided you can clearly evidence how the funds will be used. For larger purchases (above €1m), specialist borrowing options are available too with greater flexibility.”

If you’re considering using a mortgage to buy a property in Spain, it’s worth consulting a broker who specialises in overseas mortgages.

Simon Conn, an overseas mortgage broker, recommends getting a mortgage in principle before agreeing to buy the property and having an opt-out clause in case the loan falls through.

“Arrange your mortgage in the currency that you earn in when possible, but if you receive rental income in local currency, this can possibly be used to offset the monthly repayments,” he added.

What are the tax implications?

If you’re a resident of the UK but let out a property in Spain, you’ll need to pay tax on your rental income in Spain, but also declare it in the UK as part of your worldwide income.

However, you won’t pay tax twice. “Because we have a double taxation treaty, you only pay any difference between the two in the UK,” Mr Esders explained.

“There is a temptation for some people to be very selective with where they declare the income. They don’t declare the income in Spain if they rent out to other British people and get paid in Sterling, and then persuade themselves that they do not have to declare the income in the UK on the basis that the property is in Spain. Neither of those things [are] correct.”

When the property is sold, there will also be tax to pay on capital gains. “You primarily pay that in Spain on any gain that you have made, and then you also declare that in the UK, but again the double taxation treaty means that you only pay any difference in the UK,” Mr Esders said.

What pitfalls should you be aware of?

The key to making a successful investment is to get the right advice. The biggest pitfall, according to Mr Radford, is rushing into a purchase. “Agents will talk about location, location, location – but we say it’s preparation, preparation, preparation.

“For example, you might want to talk to your lawyer about ownership options to ensure your purchase is as tax-effective as possible.”

Mr Conn also stressed the importance of having an independent building survey – even if you’re looking to buy a new-build – and that you understand what you’re agreeing to. “Check the translation of what you are signing, and if it’s an ‘English version’, ensure it is a true translation of the original.”

While some buyers might just want their property to “wash its face” while they aren’t using it, if you’re relying on its investment potential, you also need to be realistic about the money it will make you.

“It is vital to go through the figures and be realistic about how much you can rent it out for. A seller may tell you that you can rent it out for €x a week – but for how many weeks a year?” said Mr Esders. “They may tell you that you can rent it out X number of weeks a year, but at what price? Generally, you will rent out less than you think you will and will make less than you think.”

Finally, be careful of where your advice comes from. As Mr Esders said: “One of the issues with Spain is that there are many people who think that they know what the legal situation is and are happy to dish out that advice on the internet but who don’t really understand and therefore end up giving bad advice.”

You might also find there’s a difference between what the law says and what people actually do, he added. “That is why it is vital to get independent professional advice from specialists who understand both Spanish and UK law and tax. We spend a lot of our time sorting out problems for people who didn’t take legal or tax advice in the first place or took bad advice from the internet.”

data.title: Rejoice! The English sports car is coming back
data.description:
Join me, if you will, for a stroll through time to the garage of memories at the end of the garden, where the pungent odour of motor oil mixes with mouldering grass cuttings.

In here you might find your father’s MGB roadster from 1968 – the one in Old English White with chrome bumpers, wire-spoke wheels and the black leather interior that reeked of Rothmans.

The canvas roof always leaked, but it was a joy to be in – even if its restrained good looks and rather limited power made it the sports car equivalent of a Rich Tea Biscuit.

Perhaps, however, the English sports car of yesteryear in your garage is the peerless Austin Healey 3000 from the 1960s – an aesthetic masterpiece, though not if it’s been rolled through a hedge (which is what my father did to his mother’s Healey after a binge at the George and Dragon in Epping).

Or how about the angular 1970s Triumph TR7? My Uncle William had one in burnt gold – which my cousin Andy used to rearrange a lamppost. William had it rebuilt. But you would.

Whichever old English sports car inhabits your own showroom of nostalgia, whether it’s one of the above or a grunty TVR Griffith or a lithe Lotus Esprit – like Roger Moore had in For Yours Eyes Only, or Julia Roberts floored in Pretty Woman – it’s a sad fact that while a few are still on the roads today, the companies that made them have either vanished or changed beyond recognition.

MG was mauled badly before being sold to the Chinese – who moved production to China in 2016. Triumph disappeared into the catastrophe of the demise of British Leyland. Lotus is owned by Geely, another Chinese giant, which announced it was cutting a third of the workforce – 550 jobs – at its HQ and factory in Norfolk last year. TVR exists and there is news of a new model… again.

Last time I heard Morgan, the venerable maker of wooden-framed sports cars from Malvern was thriving, albeit in the hands of a Continental industrial conglomerate, and then there’s Jaguar, which had the F-Type until it ceased production in 2024, though it was hardly the “spiritual successor” of the E-Type – a serious contender for the title of most beautiful sports car of all time. Aston Martin, the modern Bond’s wheels of choice, trundles on but is seemingly mired in commercial woes.

So it’s heartening that in these dark days, where good news is about as rare as a Resident Doctor on duty in England, that lovers of English sports cars have something to celebrate.

Because a new sports car start-up – Longbow – has unveiled a spankingly beautiful all-electric open-top “Speedster” model, which will do 0-60 in 3.5 seconds and allegedly go 275 miles on battery power. While the £84,995-Speedster is a rich-man’s toy – there’s no roof or windscreen – and is available only in a limited 150-model run (most of which are already sold), next year Longbow plans to begin making a Roadster model – a two-seater with a roof that will be priced at £64,000 and do 280 miles.

What’s more, they hope to make 5,000 of them a year with an entirely British supply chain, except for batteries which aren’t yet made here – but when they are, they will be.

If it all transpires, this will create and secure several thousand British jobs in the automotive sector. And it’s just the start: the firm would like to expand into bigger cars.

While I don’t think they have received a single handout from the Government, they do stand to benefit from Rachel Reeves’s economic policies because there are now quite a lot of unemployed automotive workers out there looking for new jobs.

England, says Longbow’s co-founder Mark Tapscott, who is ex-Tesla and BYD, is still the best place in the world to make a light-weight sports car, and what could be better if you’re embracing an electric future where every gram counts? Astonishingly, the Roadster weighs just 995 kilos, so about half of a Tesla Y or, funnily enough, about the same as my dad’s little old MGB.

Which is technologically impressive, and should give us all hope. Because while 5,000 cars a year – assuming they achieve it – is small beer compared with giants like Tesla, it’s a step in the right direction – and one that builds on a great English tradition of lightweight sports cars.

Not just that, it proves that despite the gloom, despite the burden of Labour’s taxes, regulation and energy costs, we can still do it. And, thank Heaven for that, because there’s no better way to explore the English countryside than behind the wheel of an English sports car. It’s what the Sussex Downs, the rolling hills of Hampshire and the Lake District were invented for.

data.title: ‘You’re destroying the economy’: how Labour killed Britain’s thriving chemical industry
data.description:
Tim Carter slows the car to a crawl, eventually pulling over to allow for the full scale of what lies out of the window to be taken in. The sprawling Wilton petrochemicals site where he works, south of the River Tees, once thrummed with activity.

Opened in 1949 by chemicals powerhouse ICI, it quickly became one of Britain’s most important technical hubs, helping to propel the North East to the vanguard of advanced manufacturing.

Yet on a warm Tuesday morning in April, the 2,000-acre complex has the feel of a graveyard.

Framed by a bright blue sky, the skeletons of once-mighty industrial giants are dotted across the horizon in every direction – fossils from an era when Britain could still rely on such production strongholds to provide the building blocks that power a civilised, modern economy.

The plant that Carter oversees as operations site manager is at risk of being added to the casualty list, further degrading Britain’s capacity to produce what industrialists call “foundational chemicals”.

These are the substances that are essential in the production of plastics, fertilisers, pharmaceuticals, industrial materials, most consumer goods and scores of other products that most of us take for granted.

“The team are trying to remain focused on running the site as well as possible, because that’s the best thing that we can do to demonstrate the efficiency, the safety and also the long-term viability,” he says, careful not to stray from the official party line.

Still, such optimism may be in vain. Owned by America’s Huntsman Corporation, Peter Huntsman, its chairman and chief executive, has warned that the company may be forced to pull the plug on what is one of its last remaining UK outposts.

The chemicals industry says Britain is ignoring the struggles of specialist factories such as Huntsman’s at its peril. With a workforce of just 80 people, its fate may seem relatively inconsequential, but the plant produces aniline, a chemical used in the production of car seats, mattresses, footwear, drugs such as paracetamol and many other everyday products.

It is also the only one of its kind left standing in the whole of the UK.

Chemicals underpin 95pc of all manufactured goods. Yet, many other critical chemical facilities similar to Huntsman’s have vanished in recent years – victims of what the Ineos billionaire Sir Jim Ratcliffe has called “the deindustrialisation of the UK”.

However, despite its significance, bosses fear the hollowing out of the UK’s industrial base is in danger of almost being missed in the corridors of Westminster.

India’s Tata Chemicals pulled down the shutters permanently on its soda ash plant in Lostock, on the outskirts of Northwich, Cheshire, last year.

The decision not only brought to an end nearly 150 years of uninterrupted manufacturing on the site, but left Britain without its own supplies of sodium carbonate, a key ingredient in the manufacture of glass, paper, detergents, and to treat fresh water.

Tata’s plans were announced in 2024 but they didn’t appear in the national press until it was picked up by Sky News earlier this year.

Grant Pearson, chairman of Ensus, one of Britain’s last bioethanol producers, warns against complacency.

“If you don’t have a chemicals industry ... then you can’t have pharmaceutical production, aerospace production or car production,” he says.

“Without a core basic chemical manufacturing base, you’d end up with those businesses either moving overseas, or these industries totally reliant on overseas supply chains.”

The conflict in Iran has once again shone a spotlight on Britain’s vulnerability to global economic shocks from an acute over-reliance on foreign markets.

But could events in the Middle East prove to be a silver lining for Britain’s industrial future? Although the energy crunch threatens to unleash a new cost of living crisis, there are tentative hopes it could also act as a long overdue wake-up call for complacent policymakers.

Huntsman believes ministers have given “zero thought” to what the loss of its Wilton factory would mean for the economy. Yet the consequences of losing a vital link in a supply chain have the potential to be devastating, he says.

He points to Ineos’s salt plant in Runcorn, Cheshire, which the firm has threatened to close. “Wait until you have to be reliant on foreign powers to get the chemistry to clean your drinking water, or to treat your food, just because of one product out of one factory. That’s what you’re down to [in the UK],” he says.

Its uncertain future sums up the severity of Britain’s industrial decline. The factory is the last of its kind in the country. If it vanishes, Britain will become a net importer of salt for the first time in hundreds of years – a humiliating new low for a nation once known as “the workshop of the world”.

‘Existential’ energy costs

On the southern boundary road of the Wilton site, a mile and half from Huntsman’s aniline plant, is one of the UK’s last remaining bioethanol facilities.

For months, the Ensus factory sat idle, having been mothballed last September after a trade deal with Washington opened the floodgates to cheap imports from the US.

But just weeks after American bombs began raining down on Tehran, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, suddenly approved a £100m plan to reopen the complex.

The Government was propelled into action over fears that Britain was facing devastating shortages of CO2 – a byproduct of bioethanol production – vital in the manufacture of fizzy drinks, food packaging, IVF treatment and the stunning of animals in abattoirs.

It was the first major ministerial intervention to prevent shortages caused by the Iran war. Robin Brooks, senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, a US think tank, said manufacturing data published last week by S&P Global laid bare the scale of the UK’s exposure compared with other countries around the world.

“High oil prices and supply chain disruptions are taking their toll,” he says. “They’re hitting small, open economies like the UK and Japan hardest, while larger economies like the US and eurozone have so far seen modest hits.”

Pearson, the chairman of Ensus, hailed the Government’s intervention as proof that the UK needs to protect its own production of vital chemicals, rather than becoming even more reliant on imports.

“Right across the chemical sector in particular, we’re seeing closures of plants, which not only impact the jobs in those sectors, but means we’re not making those basic raw materials,” he said after ministers stepped in.

Kyle, meanwhile, said the Government had “acted swiftly to boost the resilience of our supply chains and protect critical UK sectors”.

Yet for all the triumphalism, UK chemical manufacturers insist the crisis they are grappling with is one very much made at home, thanks to the highest energy costs in the developed world.

Last year, Sir Jim’s Ineos announced the permanent closure of its ethanol plant in Grangemouth, Falkirk, with the loss of 80 jobs. The facility was one of only two plants in Europe producing synthetic ethanol, which is used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, plastics and paints.

Ineos blamed a doubling of UK energy prices over the last five years, “incentivising imports from the US” where energy prices were five times lower at the time.

According to the Office for National Statistics, British industry is at the greatest disadvantage to foreign competitors when it comes to electricity bills because the price of natural gas determines the cost of electricity 98pc of the time. Business leaders have repeatedly called for this anomaly to be addressed.

Although gas prices have come down from their post-Ukraine peak, they remain considerably higher than they were before Russia’s invasion. Britain’s vulnerability is made more acute by a reliance on overseas markets for as much as 60pc of its natural gas, and a growing reliance on more expensive liquefied natural gas from the US, Qatar and Africa.

UK companies also face comparatively higher costs for distribution and the upkeep of the pylons, cables and substations that make up the network.

With energy representing more than 50pc of many manufacturers’ cost base, such disparities can quickly become “existential”, says Steve Elliott, the chief executive of the Chemicals Industry Association (CIA).

Before the crisis, Huntsman Corporation’s energy costs in Britain were three to four times what they were elsewhere. The Fortune 500 company has operations in more than 25 countries across the globe.

Once American bombs started landing on Iran, the disparity rocketed to around nine times as natural gas prices went through the roof. The chasm leaves the future of its Wilton unit hanging in the balance, Huntsman said in March.

Punishing energy bills are being compounded by Ed Miliband’s obsession with reaching arbitrary net zero targets. Companies have been left contending with a mountain of government-imposed green levies and taxes including the climate change levy, renewables obligation and feed-in tariffs.

Elliott has dubbed it “decarbonisation through deindustrialisation,” and says it is no coincidence that a near 40pc fall in UK chemical production between between 2021 and 2024 was accompanied by a similar percentage reduction in the sector’s carbon emissions.

In February, the Chemicals Industry Association issued a fresh plea for “business-supportive policy”. Elliott said the collapse in output was the result of “successive government failures to support UK manufacturing”. Policy has been tantamount to “economic vandalism”, Elliott said.

Sir Jim suggested in December that carbon taxes are fundamentally flawed because “deindustrialising Britain achieves nothing for the environment. It merely shifts production and emissions elsewhere”.

There’s an irony, too, because net zero can’t be achieved without many of the chemicals that the UK is producing much less of in the face of uncompetitive energy costs, industry figures point out.

The production of electric vehicle batteries, wind turbines, solar panels and carbon capture technology are all heavily reliant on many building-block chemicals such as ethylene, propylene, ammonia and methanol that Britain either no longer produces or where home-grown supplies are considered scarce.

The same can be said of many of the industries that the Government has declared a priority in its recently updated industrial strategy.

Domino effect

A tour around the Wilton site is a stark reminder of the consequences of the failure to level the playing field for manufacturing. Carter pulls a map from his pocket. On it are a series of red crosses representing other major facilities that have been abandoned.

Among them is the “Olefins 6” ethylene cracker, or at least what remains of it. Permanently shut down last year by its Saudi owners, engineers are already in the process of dismantling a facility that had been a feature of the Teesside skyline since it began operating almost half a century ago.

“This is all decontaminated,” Carter says, nodding to a dizzying maze of metal pipes, tubes and towers. Some of the pipes have been cut, meaning the plant can never be revived.

Olefins 6 used extreme heat to break down, or “crack”, hydrocarbons into ethylene, a raw material used by neighbouring plants.

Experts warn of a dangerous domino effect: when one facility disappears, it imperils the existence of others in close proximity because of the inter-dependency that is a common feature of the chemicals industry.

“The issue with all of this is that the industry is incredibly integrated,” Paul Hodges, a former ICI director-turned-consultant, says.

“Every time you lose one of those adjoining plants, you lose efficiency, and you lose the ability to make certain products. The main issue ... is that if you allow that plant to close, actually, you’re going to close this, this, this and this because of that inter-alliance.”

“Our industry’s biggest customer is itself,” adds Elliott, of the Chemicals Industry Association (CIA).

On the other side of the River Tees sits CF Industries’ Billingham complex, which produces ammonia. If anything captures Britain’s muddled approach to industrial policy, it is surely this.

In 2021 and again in 2022, the American company paused the machinery at Wilton, and another facility in Ince, Cheshire, citing sharp rises in natural gas prices, high carbon costs, and dwindling demand. Cheaper imports were also a factor, it said.

Ministers panicked. Ammonia is a key ingredient in the fertilisers that ensure farmers can keep the supermarket shelves stocked with food. It is also vital to the defence industry as a core component in the manufacture of munitions and explosives.

But it was the threat to carbon dioxide supplies – a byproduct of ammonia production – that prompted Boris Johnson’s government to step in with a bailout thought to be worth tens of millions of pounds to ensure the lights stayed on. CF Fertilisers was responsible for about 60pc of the UK’s commercial CO2 supplies, essential for fizzy drinks and fresh food but nuclear power too.

Yet despite the generosity of taxpayers, the company shut the Ince site permanently, and at Billingham switched to importing ammonia from the US, instead of producing it locally. Ammonia now features on a growing list of hugely important industrial chemicals that Britain obtains entirely from overseas.

Alex Cunningham, the then-Labour MP for Stockton-on-Tees, whose former constituency houses the plant, said the decision was the result of an “abject lack of action and urgency to support energy-intensive industries” on the part of ministers. “Any pretence at an industrial strategy has been abandoned,” he claimed at the time.

According to Sky News, Britain has lost 11 critical factories in the space of a decade.

The closures are part of a wider crunch that has resulted in the disappearance of 25 chemical plants in the last five years alone, the CIA calculates. This rapid retrenchment has left Britain with either no production at all or “limited” domestic output of 11 basic yet vital chemicals including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, and sulphuric acid.

Last month, in a letter to the Prime Minister, the CIA warned that the decline in chemical output reached 60pc last year. The Association says the sector is now facing “the fight of its life”.

‘We need urgent action’

There are tentative signs that the Government is listening.

In December, it stepped in with a £125m support package to help save the last remaining ethylene factory in Grangemouth. A reported £100m to protect CO2 supplies at Ensus in Teesside is viewed among some industry figures as further proof that ministers are beginning to grasp what is at stake.

The Department for Business and Trade said the reopening of the Ensus plant formed part of “wider government work” designed to ensure the UK maintained access to critical industrial resources during global supply shocks.

Meanwhile, a spokesman for the Government said it was working with industry on potential solutions to the Middle East energy crisis.

They said: “We’re moving full steam ahead to tackle this which is why we’ve taken action to cut electricity costs for industries across Great Britain.”

On the other hand, there are plenty of recent examples of critical industrial capacity – not just in chemicals – being allowed to vanish with barely a whimper from Labour officials.

Grangemouth oil refinery, on the Firth of Forth, and Prax Lindsey refinery, on the banks of the Humber estuary, both closed for good last year, knocking out a third of the UK’s refining capacity and leaving households and businesses even more exposed to global price shocks.

The remaining four refineries could soon follow. A cross-party group of MPs has warned that those left standing face the risk of closure because they are included in the UK Emissions Trading Scheme – a carbon tax that ratchets up annually. But as they point out, foreign refineries face no such taxes, enabling them to increasingly undercut rivals here.

The levy “risks further refinery closures and at worst the UK becoming entirely dependent on imported fuel at a time of unprecedented global instability and geopolitical risk”, Elizabeth de Jong, the chief executive of Fuels Industry UK, said.

“This is a political decision and the Treasury is knowingly increasing the competitive disadvantage faced by our remaining refineries. Ministers say they value energy security and the UK’s industrial base, but now we need urgent action,” she added.

Elsewhere, India’s Tata has been cajoled into turning off its blast furnaces in Port Talbot, South Wales, in the name of making the planet greener, with hundreds of millions of pounds of taxpayers’ money. The move cost 2,000 local steelworkers their jobs. Similar plans are afoot at British Steel in Scunthorpe, Lincolnshire, though there it has chosen to protect jobs through nationalisation.

Huntsman says the decisions sum up how muddled industrial and energy policies are in Britain. The idea that “shutting down steel mills” and “moving capacity to India ... is an environmental victory is astounding,” he says.

“You’re destroying the economy, your tax base and your regulatory base,” Huntsman adds.

“You’re shipping these jobs and the CO2 elsewhere to countries that are far worse regulated, so you’re destroying the environment too. Yet people are patting themselves on the back, saying that they’re cleaning up the UK.”

Risk to jobs

Instead of slowing the charge towards net zero in the face of the Middle East crisis, Miliband and the Government have pledged to double down on renewables.

Calls to make Britain less reliant on foreign imports by fast-tracking new drilling licences in major North Sea fields such as Jackdaw and Rosebank have been dismissed. Instead, Labour believes the Iran energy shock is further proof that Britain needs to continue to move away from fossil fuels.

The Government has stepped in with some relief from energy costs. The Energy Intensive Industries scheme – conceived in 2016 – has been updated to include greater relief from some net zero levies for high-energy manufacturers.

The more recent British industrial competitiveness scheme cuts electricity bills for manufacturers by 25pc, but the bar for eligibility is high and applicants must pass a whole series of tests first. Even then, it doesn’t kick in for another year.

Huntsman dismisses such initiatives as “whistling past the graveyard”. “So you’re going to take all these taxes that would have put us out of business and suspend them for the short-term?” he asks.

He points to a recent report from Roland Berger, a management consultancy, that showed an 81pc fall in capital spend among Europe’s chemicals producers.

“If you’ve got a vehicle and you’re suspending 81pc of your maintenance of that vehicle, that means the vehicle is being driven until the wheels literally fall off,” says Huntsman. “They’re putting just enough money in to keep it running until the next major issue comes up and then it’s down for good, and when these things [factories] go down, they stay down.”

In Teesside, one of Carter’s great frustrations is that Huntsman Corporation has spent tens of millions of pounds making its Teesside plant more energy-efficient. “We are as efficient as possible but because of the raw material costs that are feeding through, we can’t remain competitive. It’s not a level playing field,” he says.

Mustapha, a local taxi driver whose grandfather was employed at the nearby British Steel site before it closed in 2015, is concerned about the effect of seemingly endless factory closures on an area he has lived in for 26 years.

Could the thousands of jobs being lost to deindustrialisation be replaced by employment on green projects, as Labour claims?

South Korean steel specialist SeAH opened a wind power components factory down the road last year, but Mustapha is doubtful the sectors of the future can bring the same benefits as the heavy industry of old.

“Those jobs are for the educated few. The steelworks provided jobs for everybody. Look at those on the right,” he says pointing to a row of crumbling red brick buildings on the short journey to Wilton. “All this was built for the British Steel people.”

He remembers a pizza takeaway that used to serve the steelworkers. “They made thousands [of pounds] a night ... but that business is finished.” In Labour’s hands, the same may soon be said of British industry.
