data.title: London’s house price plunge is a warning sign for the whole economy
data.description:
Prices in Westminster are down by 12pc over the last year. In Kensington and Chelsea they are down by 11.2pc and by just as much in the City of London.

Over the last few weeks, it has become clear that house prices in the swankiest parts of London are in freefall. Plenty of people will be relaxed about that, and a few might even celebrate it if it means that living in the capital becomes more affordable. There is just one catch. It has the potential to blow up the whole economy – and a housing crash is the last thing Britain needs right now.

Anyone who thought that an apartment, or even better a whole house, in one of the smarter parts of London was the safest possible investment, or indeed a guarantee of lavish retirement, will have had a rude shock if they have checked Rightmove recently.

Prices are collapsing. According to the latest data from the Office for National Statistics, prices in all London’s most affluent boroughs are suffering from double-digit declines, and have now been falling for five consecutive months.

They are still expensive by any normal standards. In Kensington and Chelsea, the average price is still £1.2m, and in Westminster £872,000.

But they have suffered the biggest slump since the 2008-09 financial crash, and are now back to 2013 levels. In effect, a whole decade of gains has been wiped out, and if you were unlucky enough to have bought at anything close to the peak you will now be sitting on a big loss by now.

In fairness, across the rest of the country, there is not going to be a lot of sympathy. Prices in central London were wildly overvalued anyway. Many of the properties were owned by rich foreigners, who hardly even bothered to visit them very often. The speculators can afford the losses. It might even make it easier for first-time buyers to find somewhere to live. If anything, we should be celebrating housing becoming a little more affordable. It will make life easier for everyone.

Perhaps. The trouble is, it could also turn into a huge problem for the wider British economy. There are four big issues.

To start with, it is the most direct confirmation we have that the wealthy are fleeing London. It is the only explanation for the sudden falls: people want to leave and they don’t mind taking a loss on the way out. They might not be going to Dubai any more, but they are going somewhere else instead. Closing the non-dom scheme, raising capital gains taxes and the highest inheritance tax in the developed world have driven them out of the capital.

The class warriors in Labour and the Green Party might welcome that. Bashing the rich is their favourite occupation. But as you lose the wealthiest people, you also lose all the jobs they create and the investment they bring into the country as well. It is hardly good for the economy.

Next, it is going to turn into a huge drain on the Treasury. All those Westminster and Chelsea apartments were paying 12pc in stamp duty and often more if the owners were foreign or if it was a second home. Stamp duty brings in £15bn a year, and on some estimates more than half of that came from the top band on properties above £1.5m.

At a rough estimate, a collapse in the London market would cost Rachel Reeves between £6bn and £7bn a year, and since this Government refuses to control spending, that money would have to be found from higher borrowing or higher taxes on the rest of us. As for the planned mansion tax on properties worth more than £2m, perhaps we should scrap it now. At this rate, there won’t be enough left to make it worth the hassle.

Thirdly, it will trap lots of people in negative equity. Anyone who bought at any time over the last decade, especially if they took out a big mortgage, as most of them probably did, will now be stuck with a property that is worth less than the debt secured on it.

They won’t be able to move even if they can sell their home, because they will have no way of repaying the loan. If they lose their job and can’t keep up repayments, the mortgage lender will have to take the hit. Just as seriously, through the Help To Buy scheme, the Government may well face heavy losses on its own loans, and that will leave the public finances in even worse shape than they already are.

Finally, price drops in central London are often the first signal of wider falls across the country. House prices nationally have held up remarkably well so far despite the steep rise in interest rates, all the extra taxes, and a stagnating economy. But who says that is going to last?

Anyone who is quietly pleased about the losses suffered on a swanky apartment in Chelsea won’t be quite so happy when very ordinary homes in Bristol or Newcastle are witnessing double-digit price declines. A general house price crash always starts somewhere. You have to be very optimistic to think that falling prices in central London won’t be rippling out to the rest of the country by the end of the year.

In reality, a property crash is the last thing the British economy needs right now. The economy is already close to a recession. Inflation is poised to rise following the surge in oil prices in the wake of the war in Iran leaving the Bank of England with no room to cut interest rates.

The Chancellor is spending so wildly that taxes will inevitably have to rise again in the autumn. There are few jobs being created, and investment is evaporating. It is a dismal outlook.

If house prices start to slide, it would crush consumer spending, and pose a threat to the financial system as well. It hasn’t happened yet – but unfortunately the signals from Westminster and Kensington suggest that it could be on the way.

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine lives’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: London office construction hits 10-year low in blow to Starmer
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s hopes of getting Britain building have been dealt a major setback after office construction in London plunged 35pc to its lowest level in a decade.

Construction work began on around 4.8 million square feet of office space in the capital in 2025, according to Deloitte’s latest London office crane survey. It found the number of new-build office projects more than halved year-on-year to just 1.6 million square feet of space.

Deloitte warned that the slump in building work risked leaving the UK with a shortage of office space as soon as 2027.

Philip Parnell, the head of real estate valuation at Deloitte, said the downturn had been driven by the “complexity involved in navigating the planning system”. He said developers were also struggling to “manage and mitigate” rising build costs.

Just one of the five biggest developments that began last year – a 24-storey office tower in the City of London, known as the Dovetail Building – was a new-build project. The remaining four consisted of refurbishments.

The data follow warnings that building new schemes in London has become especially difficult for property developers.

Mr Parnell said developers were more cautious about whether new projects would be financially viable and whether they could deliver them as they grappled with growing red tape.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise, the chief executive of Next, warned this week that the country’s planning system was the “biggest drag on British prosperity”.

He proposed that the entire rulebook for planning should be rewritten in favour of a market-based approach, adding: “As with some terrible buildings, sometimes we have to tear down and build again because planned economies don’t work.”

The slowdown in office construction in London will come as a blow to the Prime Minister, who came to power promising the “biggest building boom in a generation” through planning reforms. He pledged to “take an axe to red tape”.

However, since then, the number of new homes built has also fallen to a decade-low.

Mr Parnell said: “Developers still see a planning process as complex and expensive and time-consuming.”

He warned the situation risked worsening as builders braced for a rise in construction costs from the war in Iran.

Mr Parnell said: “There is more than just a precautionary note here around build-cost inflation and initially that does present a real risk and challenge to delivery.”

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

The great savings rate decline may not happen this year, as war in the Middle East has halted plans for further Bank Rate reductions. While this is good news for your savings, it’s tempered by the fact that inflation is expected to rise steeply, eating away at your returns. 

To protect your money from its damaging effects, it’s worth putting some effort into finding the accounts paying the best rates possible.

But with so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can afford to lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Katie Horne of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Katie says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Al Rayan Bank 12-month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.65pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,720 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is Chetwood Bank’s 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 4.4pc AER, which would generate £3,520 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – Paragon Bank Instant Access Cash Isa pays 3.76pc AER, generating interest of £752 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose the Whiteaway Laidlaw (part of Shawbrook Bank) instant access account, paying 3.94pc AER, earning £788 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.27pc to 4.5pc, equivalent to between £4,272 and £4,508 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Katie says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Whiteaway Laidlaw, which pays 3.94pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,152 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the Gatehouse Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.11pc AER, earning £3,288 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the Chetwood Bank 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4.4pc, earning £880 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.65pc with the Al Rayan Bank UK 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £930 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-18 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.03pc to 4.21pc, or £4,032 to £4,218, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Katie says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can currently invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.51pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £89 a year.”

Scenario 4

A saver in their 60s with £100,000 in cash. They don’t need access to all of his money immediately, but want to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Katie says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Whiteaway Laidlaw, paying 3.94pc AER, earning £985 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a six-month fixed term account from the Gatehouse Bank, paying 4.34pc AER, earning £1,085 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Al Rayan Bank, paying 4.65pc, which would generate £1,162.50 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from Jordan International Bank, paying 4.31pc AER, earning £1,077.50 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.31pc, generating around £4,310 in annual interest, with funds maturing every six to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Least effective Tube strike for years as union loses its grip
data.description:
The RMT union’s Tube strike has been its least effective walkout for years, according to official data.

More than half of scheduled services operated normally across all London Underground (LU) lines on Tuesday, despite the 24-hour walkout.

This is a significant contrast to the union’s last strike in September, when all lines were suspended.

That Tube services were maintained despite the industrial action suggests that, for the first time in many years, the militant RMT has lost its grip.

Thomas Turrell, the Conservatives’ City Hall transport spokesman, hailed the news as a “bitter disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London”.

“Whilst it is a relief that the full damage of this strike did not come to pass, we must not ignore the fact that these strikes come only because the unions think that our weak Labour Mayor will capitulate to them,” he warned.

Around 60 per cent of Tube drivers turned up for work on Tuesday, an LU source confirmed.

Other sources said they could not remember the last time an RMT strike had resulted in more than half of planned services running.

All 10,000 of the RMT’s LU members walked out in September, in contrast to this week’s strikes where only drivers refused to work.

Many had believed that a Tube-wide RMT drivers’ strike could force most trains to be cancelled.

Yet, just under two-thirds of the usual service level operated on the Bakerloo, Victoria and Northern lines during Tuesday’s strike, LU told The Telegraph.

The Jubilee and Metropolitan lines kept up more than half of their normal timetable, even though the latter was part-suspended between Baker Street and Aldgate.

Londoners generally heeded warnings from Transport for London (TfL) that “significant disruption” was expected, with officials recording a 13 per cent drop in the number of contactless cards tapping in on Tuesday compared with the same day last year.

This equates to around half a million fewer travellers, having decreased from 3.9 million tap-ins last year to 3.4 million earlier this week.

Passenger numbers were also down 8 per cent on the Overground and 13 per cent on the DLR. Neither line is affected by strikes, although many use these services to change to the Tube.

Around 4 per cent more people used the Elizabeth Line than usual, however, reflecting the part-suspension of the Central Line across its middle stretch between White City and Liverpool Street. The Elizabeth Line parallels the Central as it passes through the middle of the capital.

Fewer Tube drivers members of RMT

The second of this week’s 24-hour walkouts will end at 12pm on Friday, with transport bosses warning that Tube trains will not restart until 7.30am at the earliest.

Around half of London’s 3,300 Tube drivers are members of the RMT. This figure used to be significantly higher but many have either given up their union membership or joined the rival Aslef union.

The RMT’s strikes this week were called over what it described as the “imposing” of a four-day working week on Tube drivers. Union bosses say this will result in longer shifts of up to eight hours and 45 minutes.

Aslef secured Tube bosses’ agreement to a 35-hour week, which is being offered to all drivers on a voluntary basis. The RMT, however, wants drivers’ working hours cut to 32 per week with no reduction in pay.

Mr Turrell said: “It will be a disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London that their own shortcomings have prevented them bringing the city to a standstill.

“The Conservatives created legislation to ensure this level of service as a legal minimum during strikes, which Labour promptly dropped – and as such, any strike which is more damaging to London than the one we have seen this week will be a blatant political choice by authorities.”

An RMT spokesman said: “Our strike is solid and reflects our growing presence in the driver grade in London Underground.

“TfL need to focus on revising the offer our members have overwhelmingly rejected to avert further disruption in the coming months.”

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in May
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in May 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: Best regular savings accounts for 2026: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Regular savings accounts are set up on the basis that you’ll be contributing little and often. Some may even require you to commit to paying in a certain amount of money each month – commonly between £10 and £500.

Not only are these accounts good for getting into the habit of saving, but you’ll usually be given a higher interest rate for your efforts.

However, they can come with strict terms. Some accounts may punish missed monthly payments with a lower rate of interest, while others won’t allow any withdrawals for a fixed-term. Many accounts also only last for a limited time, so it’s important to check the terms of each account before you sign up.

In this guide, Telegraph money will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best regular savings accounts for May
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account
  • Are regular savings accounts worth it?
  • Regular savings FAQs

How we determine the best rates

Telegraph Money’s Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best regular savings accounts for May

Use the table below to discover the best regular savings accounts for you.

For other savings options that allow you to access your money, see our guides to the best easy-access savings accounts, along with the best notice accounts and best current accounts.

You might be able to find higher rates by locking your cash away – don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Plus, don’t miss our guide to the best cash Isas, which shows the top options for easy-access, notice, regular and fixed-rates – where your savings interest is all tax-free.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Regular savers can have a very catchy headline rate. But quite often, the maximum amount of money you can deposit is low. Banks may attract you in with a high interest rate and then you realise you can save £5,000.”

“They are very good if you want to deposit small amounts of money and if you’re new to saving,” he added.

Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Regular savings accounts are an ideal choice for those wanting to receive a higher rate of interest or wish to develop and maintain a regular savings habit.

“However, many regular savings accounts don’t give much flexibility on the amount that can be deposited each month and often have minimum or maximum monthly payments.”

Are regular savings accounts worth it?

This really depends on the kind of saver you are, and what you want from an account. Regular savings accounts can be useful if you think you can reliably put away some money every month – which you might not be able to access until the account matures.

If this suits you, you can earn a relatively high rate of interest on what you save, and you’ll hopefully end up with a healthy sum if you stick to your savings habit.

On the other hand, depending on your disposable income, some might find the amounts you’re allowed to save restrictive – and they’re also no good if you’re looking for a home for a lump sum.

What’s more, while the headline interest rates might sound high, you might be surprised to see the interest you earn amounting to fairly little, as deposit restrictions will keep your balance low.

Regular savings FAQs

Yes, you can open more than one account – though some providers might have their own restrictions. Regular savers are for making smaller payments, so there is nothing stopping you from opening several to earn more interest on your money.

Some accounts will have a minimum number of payments you need to make each year – often 10 or 11. Some will require you to pay in a certain amount every month.

Yes, money held in regular savings accounts is protected as long as your account is with a UK-regulated bank or building society, the money is protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme up to £120,000 per institution.

Yes, you can open a regular savings account for your children. Check our guide to the best children’s savings accounts to see the latest rates.

A regular savings account can help develop good saving habits. If you are only able to put a small amount of money away each month and would prefer a short-term arrangement, then it might be the best situation for you.

Pros:

  • These accounts can help you get into the savings habit, which can benefit you for the rest of your life.
  • A regular savings account gives you considerably better interest rates than standard accounts, rewarding you for making frequent savings over a year or so.
  • They are inclusive – you’re usually limited to saving relatively small amounts, so those with less to save can still benefit.

Cons:

  • There are generally rigid terms and conditions. The amount you can save is often capped and your interest rate might be reduced if you don’t save every month or if you need to make a withdrawal. Many accounts don’t allow early withdrawals whatsoever.
  • You usually need to have another product with the same bank – usually a current account.
  • The rate sometimes lasts for a limited time, often a year, meaning your money is moved to an account with lower interest rates at the end of the term.
  • Some accounts have variable interest rates – meaning the provider can change the rates whenever they want – so it’s important to keep an eye on messages from the provider and switch, if the rates drop too low.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Five changes every landlord must prepare for in 2026
data.description:
Sign up to Buy-to-let Masterclass, The Telegraph’s new five-part newsletter, for actionable advice and practical tools to make the most of your property investment.

Britain’s landlords must brace for a year of new rules, as the Government plans to enforce a raft of regulations and additional tax requirements on a sector already burdened by rising costs.

While the Government insists the changes are about raising standards and strengthening protection for tenants, landlords warn that it could lead to worse outcomes for renters if enough landlords decide to leave the market entirely.

Here, Telegraph Money sets out what’s coming for landlords this year and what you need to do to get prepared.

Making Tax Digital

When? From April 6.

What does it mean for landlords?

Making Tax Digital (MTD) requires certain taxpayers to report their income and expenses to HMRC four times a year, in addition to annual tax returns. This doesn’t only affect landlords – other taxpayers, such as the self-employed, are also affected.

For landlords specifically, MTD applies to those whose qualifying income exceeds £50,000, but this income doesn’t have to be from property. For example, if you have income from property and self-employment that is more than £50,000 combined, then you would need to register.

Those required to take action should have already received a letter from HMRC, but, as always, the onus is on you to check whether you should be signed up.

Heather Powell, of accountancy firm Blick Rothenberg, said: “The first return, reporting rental income and expenses for the three months from April 6, 2026 to July 5, 2026 has to be filed by Aug 7, 2026, with filing from ‘compliant software’ or bridging software that picks up the financial information from an Excel spreadsheet.”

As a landlord, you need to be on your game, as there is an initial £200 fine after two late filings.

While HMRC says this new filing regime will “increase the accuracy of filings”, the shift will undoubtedly feel daunting for the many landlords juggling multiple properties and income streams.

What’s more, the submissions must be made using MTD-compatible software. There is a long list of companies offering options, many of which require payment. Whether landlords try to grapple with this on their own or with the help of an accountant, they’re likely to incur additional costs.

The key right now is to find out whether you’re affected and, if you are, get your accounting records in order and start moving towards digital record-keeping.

Renters’ Rights Act reforms come into force

When? May 1.

What does it mean for landlords?

After becoming law in November, the first phase of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) 2025 reforms will come into effect from the start of May – and there’s a lot to get your head around.

Michael Dear, landlord insurance specialist at insurer Hiscox, said: “The RRA brings changes such as limits on rent increases and the end of fixed-term tenancies, which aim to make renting fairer.”

In addition, there are new rules on “no-fault” evictions and tenant requests to keep pets.

Sián Hemming-Metcalfe, operations director at Inventory Base, a supplier, said: “Most landlords know the RRA is coming, but far fewer have grasped what it means in practice. This is not a cosmetic change; it rewrites how possession works, how rent is handled, how tenancies are structured and what landlords will need to evidence if something goes wrong.”

As such, there are a few things you should prepare now, if you haven’t already.

  • Before the abolition of Section 21: Review the new rules in detail and potentially seek legal advice if you are likely to need to evict tenants.
  • Prepare for the end of fixed-term tenancies: All existing and new fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies must convert into rolling tenancies, so think now about how you need to change your existing processes and update your contract paperwork.
  • Prepare for rent increases being restricted to once per year: Now is a good time to assess how much rent you’re charging. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, said: “With rent increases due to be open to tribunal challenge from May, many landlords are using the months ahead to ensure their rents are aligned as closely as possible to market levels.”
  • Plan for new pet rules: Requests to keep pets cannot be turned down “unreasonably” starting in May. Before this, it’s prudent to consider any practical adjustments that might better protect your property from pet damage, update your inventories and perhaps your tenancy agreement paperwork and insurance policy to make sure you’re covered.

For more information on what the new law means for landlords, see our full guide to the Renters’ Rights Act.

Provide information to existing tenants

When? By May 31 to avoid penalties.

What does it mean for landlords?

This is part of the RRA, but has its own deadline, so we thought it was worth pointing out separately.

The requirement is for landlords to issue The Renters’ Rights Act Information Sheet, either in physical form or as a downloadable pdf in an email, to tenants with existing tenancy agreements.

Tenants renting without a tenancy agreement cannot be sent this, and instead must be provided with key information in writing that sets out the details of the tenancy – including, but not restricted to:

  • The property address
  • The tenancy start date
  • The rent amount and when it is due
  • A statement to confirm that any proposed rent rises will be in accordance with section 13 of the Housing Act 1988
  • Details of the tenancy deposit
  • Requirements should apply if either party wishes to end the tenancy.

New tenancies made on or after May 1 must include this information as standard.

Don’t ignore this, as failure to comply will be considered a breach of the act and can mean hefty penalties of up to £7,000.

You can read the full government guidance for landlords here.

Further ahead: New Energy Performance Certificate metrics

When? Had been planned for the end of 2026, but recently delayed to 2027.

What does it mean for landlords?

Under current plans, landlords must ensure that all rental properties have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of “C” or above by 2030 or spend £10,000 to try.

However, the existing EPC system is also due for reform, with the single metric to be replaced by four new metrics, which had been expected in the latter half of this year. The Government has since announced the new system will not be introduced until 2027.

Once introduced, landlords will have to achieve a rating of “C” in the property’s “fabric performance” (which considers insulation) and least two of the other three new green metrics.

The aim is to help landlords (as well as other homeowners and renters) understand certain headline measures in their properties. The assessments will also cover the heating system (encouraging the adoption of heat pumps), the building’s “smart-readiness” (the potential to integrate smart technology) and practical steps to improve homes and reduce energy costs.

The idea is that this will provide a broader picture of a property’s energy profile.

The Government estimates that the average landlord will need to spend £5,400 to meet the standard, so if you know your rental property or properties have a poor EPC rating, it’s worth planning now.

You may need to start setting money aside to fund improvements and start looking into grants you can apply for (such as the Boiler Upgrade Scheme if you are considering a heat pump).

Jason Harris-Cohen, managing director at LandlordBuyer, said: “This represents a significant retrofit challenge for landlords, many of whom face difficult decisions between absorbing costly upgrade expenses, raising rents, or exiting the market altogether.”

Minimum qualifications for letting agents

When? To be confirmed.

What does it mean for landlords?

Last year, the Government set out proposals to introduce mandatory qualifications for letting agents to raise professional standards across the sector.

The plans include a new code of practice establishing minimum requirements for all residential property agents, including estate agents, managing agents and letting agents.

As yet, no date has been confirmed. However, with many landlords reliant on agents to meet increasingly complex obligations, there is growing pressure for clarity on when the reforms will take effect.

Until this happens, any landlords using letting agents to manage their properties should ensure that the agents are trained and prepared for the forthcoming law changes. For instance, is there a named compliance specialist available to answer questions about the new rules? Are there facilities for staff to be trained on the changes and any updates? Are they updating their services and terms in accordance with the new rules?

If not, you might want to consider moving to a different company, as a letting agent’s failure to follow the new law will embroil you as the landlord.

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Best fixed-rate bonds for May: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Fixed-rate bonds are savings accounts that lock away your funds for a set period of time, paying a guaranteed rate of interest throughout this term.

They can be a shrewd move in times when savings rates are on the way down – as they are now – since the rate you fix at will be unaffected until the term ends.

However, you’ll need to be sure you won’t need the cash in the meantime. Many accounts simply won’t let you access your savings until the term is over, while those that do allow early withdrawals will usually deduct your interest as a penalty.

This guide will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best fixed-rate bonds in May
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond
  • Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds
  • Fixed-rate bond FAQs

What are fixed-rate bonds?

Fixed-rate bonds may also be referred to as fixed-rate savings accounts, or even fixed-term deposits – depending on the provider. In essence, they are savings accounts that pay a set amount of interest, and last for a set amount of time.

They commonly span from nine months to five years and, in theory, the longer the term you commit to, the higher the interest rate you receive.

However, this is not always the case – during times when rates are expected to fall over the longer term, savings providers will increase rates for shorter-term bonds in a bid to avoid paying over the odds in future.

You will often need to pay in a lump sum at the start of the term, rather than making regular deposits, so these accounts tend to suit those with larger amounts to save.

The interest can be paid annually, quarterly or monthly, depending on your provider. In some cases, interest may only be paid at the end of the term – if you have a large amount saved over a number of years, receiving all the interest in one go could mean you will exceed your personal savings allowance and have to pay tax on your returns.

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update every day.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best fixed-rate bonds in May

Use the table below to explore your options for a fixed-rate deal.

For the top rates where you can access your cash whenever you like, see our guide to the best easy access accounts and the best current accounts.

Alternatively, our guide to the best notice accounts can show you the top accounts that require you to wait a little while before your money is released.

While you’re saving, you might be keen to shield your money from a potential tax bill – our guide to the best cash Isas can help you find a tax-efficient account.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond

Rachel Springall, of the analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Picking the right fixed bond term entirely depends on someone’s circumstances and how soon they wish to access their pot. There are bonds with fixed terms of just a few months, so these might be useful for someone who has a big lump sum but may need to use it before the year is over, such as a deposit for a home.

“There are fixed bonds available that pay interest monthly, which may be suited to customers looking to supplement their income.”

She also warned customers to consider the personal savings allowance (PSA) when it came to accounts that were not Isas, as there could be tax to pay on the interest you earn.

Fixed-rate bonds are worth considering for anyone who wants certainty in their returns – this can be particularly handy in times of volatility.

While it may be tempting to stick to the high street brands you’re familiar with, it’s not always the route to the best accounts.

“As it stands, some of the top rates available are from challenger banks, which should not be overlooked,” Ms Springall added.

Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds

While Premium Bonds and fixed-rate bonds might sound similar, they are very different:

  • Premium Bonds are only provided by National Savings & Investments (NS&I), while fixed-rate bonds are offered by a number of savings providers.
  • Premium Bonds aren’t savings accounts; you don’t get paid interest. Instead, each £1 bond is entered into a monthly prize draw, where you could win between £25-£1m. Fixed-rate bonds pay a set amount of interest for the duration of the term.
  • Premium Bonds prizes are paid tax-free; savings interest from fixed-rate bonds is taxable unless it’s a fixed-rate cash Isa.

Fixed-rate bond FAQS

You might have to pay tax on the interest your fixed-rate bond earns if it exceeds your tax-free allowance, unlike the money stored in an Isa.

This allowance depends on your tax bracket; basic-rate taxpayers can earn £1,000 in interest tax-free but the limit is £500 for higher-rate taxpayers and no personal savings allowance for additional-rate taxpayers.

Those with incomes of less than £17,570 may be able to benefit from the additional starting rate for savings, which gives those on lower incomes up to £5,000 as an extra tax-free savings allowance.

If you usually pay tax by self-assessment, you will be asked to detail the amount of savings interest you have earned over the tax year. If you pay tax via Pay As You Earn (PAYE), any tax on savings interest is usually calculated automatically and your tax code may be adjusted if you have tax to pay.

You must submit a tax return if you earn more than £10,000 in savings interest – regardless of whether or not you usually file one.

This will depend on what rates are available at the time you are looking for a bond, as well as whether the account suits your circumstances.

The best way to determine how “good” an interest rate is, is to see how it compares with other similar types of bond on the market. If it can easily be beaten by other providers, it might be worth looking elsewhere.

Our table of the best fixed-rate bonds above shows some of the top deals currently available.

NS&I’s Premium Bonds are not fixed-rate bonds – and they work slightly differently to other types of savings accounts.

They do not pay any interest, instead every £1 you have invested is assigned its own Premium Bonds number, which is entered into a monthly prize draw, whether you could win between £25 and £1m. Our guide to Premium Bonds explains how they work in more detail.

NS&I does offer fixed-rate bonds, including Guaranteed Growth Bonds and Guaranteed Income Bonds – see our guide to NS&I savings accounts to find out more.

data.title: Co-op uses invisible spray to catch shoplifters
data.description:
Co-op is secretly marking items with invisible forensic spray to catch shoplifters reselling them on the black market.

The supermarket said that the spray, which is also used by the police to tackle bike theft, had been trialled on commonly stolen items, including alcohol, laundry detergent, sweets, energy drinks and coffee.

Forensic spray coats items with an invisible liquid that carries a unique code visible only under UV light, allowing police to track whatever has been stolen.

It is the latest attempt to combat a shoplifting epidemic across Britain after offences surged by 133pc in the past four years.

The invisible spray is part of the Co-op’s £250m investment package to tackle retail crime. Other security measures include staff body cameras, security guards and reinforced kiosks for spirits and tobacco.

The supermarket is one of several to install special shelving kits that prevent shoplifters from sweeping entire shelves into a bag and walking out.

Paul Gerrard, the supermarket’s policy director, told The Guardian that the measures made it harder for shoplifters to resell items.

He added: “This is not about an extra avocado going into an M&S bag. That is not the reason we are seeing crime at these levels; it is about people taking out an entire meat section for resale.”

The supermarket said its investment in anti-shoplifting measures had resulted in a 46pc reduction in retail crime incidents.

Mr Gerrard said: “Shopworkers have had to tolerate unacceptable levels of theft, abuse and violence for far too long, and it’s not right.

“Local shops are an anchor in communities, and that is why Co-op, my colleagues and our members spoke up, often when others wouldn’t, to clearly say criminal behaviour, violence and abuse should not be part of the job.”

His comments come after Labour’s Crime and Policing Act received royal assent on Wednesday.

The act enshrines into law a specific offence for assaulting a retail worker, and repeals a previous measure which downgraded the police responses to “low-value” theft of items worth less than £200.

Sir Keir Starmer said this week that the “tide could be turning” on shoplifters, adding there had been a 17pc rise in the number of people charged with the offence.

He called on supermarkets to share CCTV footage with police forces immediately after a theft, adding that “the hope of technology” could make a difference.

data.title: BP considers abandoning the North Sea
data.description:
BP is considering abandoning the North Sea as its new chief executive orders a review of oil and gas operations hit by high windfall taxes.

Meg O’Neill, who took charge of the energy giant last month, is reportedly mulling a sale of all or part of its offshore operations that would fetch up to £2bn and could pave the way for a move to the US stock market.

No decisions have been made on a sell-off, which would mean the departure of one of the last oil majors operating in UK waters and end the company’s more than 60-year presence in the North Sea.

The revelation comes just days after Ed Miliband described the company’s profits as “morally and economically wrong”.

The Energy Secretary’s comments on X, which he later deleted, followed what BP called “exceptional” earnings of £2.4bn between January and March, linked to oil trading on the back of the Iran conflict.

A BP spokesman refused to comment on the prospect of a UK-wide review, reported by Bloomberg.

“We have a strong North Sea portfolio with significant untapped potential, supported by a highly skilled workforce. We don’t comment on market speculation, our focus is safe and reliable operations,” the company said.

BP has, however, been critical of the windfall taxes imposed on UK offshore operators by the Government, which other operators have linked with their decisions to leave or slash investment.

BP has already sold some assets such as its Shearwater field, Magnus platform and the Forties pipeline system, while Shell has already moved all its UK offshore assets into Adura, a 50-50 joint venture with Equinor. Others, such as Chevron Corp and ConocoPhillips, have sold assets to smaller independents.

BP’s latest tax report said the windfall tax had “created significant uncertainty for the UK’s oil and gas industry”.

Its latest annual report, covering 2025, said Labour’s decision to increase the tax by 3pc to an effective overall rate of 78pc and to extend it from 2028 to 2030 had cost the company an extra £539m.

BP is one of Britain’s top corporate taxpayers. In 2024, it paid £1.2bn in taxes to the UK Treasury.

Ms O’Neill and Albert Manifold, BP’s chairman, have pledged to reverse years of underperformance and turbulence which have seen the company go through three chief executives since 2020.

The chief executive recently told analysts that every part of BP needs to be asking: “What are the assets that are with us for the long term and what are things that might be of greater value in someone else’s hands?”

However, she has also said the North Sea still has significant potential.

“The opportunity for us is to work more closely with governments to unlock potential in countries like the UK, for example, where we have untapped potential in the North Sea,” she told the Financial Times this week.

BP holds interests in 20 to 25 producing fields in the UK, centred around several major hubs.

Its Glen Lyon, Clair Ridge and Clair Phase 1 platforms lie to the west of Shetland. Clair has an estimated seven billion barrels of oil in place, making it the largest oilfield on the UK continental shelf.

The Eastern Trough Area Project is in the northern North Sea, east of Aberdeen, and is also a key producer with the Seagull and Murlach fields opening in the last three years.

Separately, BP has confirmed plans to sell most of its stake in Net Zero Power, a project to build a gas turbine power plant on Teesside that would be the first equipped with carbon capture technology.

It is also preparing to sell a 20pc stake in Northern Endurance Partnership, a scheme to transport carbon dioxide from carbon-capture projects in north-east England and store it under the seabed in the North Sea.

Ashley Kelty, an energy analyst at investment bank Panmure Gordon, said the review was no surprise – and could even be a preparation for the company to relist in New York.

“I think there are no ‘sacred cows’ in BP that won’t be looked at for potential sale. It would make sense to explore a sale even if it never happens,” he said.

“If there is a sell-off, it will be embarrassing for the UK Government to have a major that has no interest in the UK but it would also make it easier to justify taking a market listing to, say, New York,” he said.

Dan Slater, a research director at Zeus Capital, said Britain’s crushing tax regime meant BP could get a better return by investing overseas.

“BP is a global company with the majority of its operations abroad, so it has numerous options to invest its capital outside the UK,” he said. 

“Any exit from the UK North Sea would be another consequence of the Government’s inappropriate tax and unhelpful regulatory policies.”

Richard Tice, Reform UK’s energy spokesman, said: “They’re basically saying stuff you – we can take our investments and our jobs elsewhere if you’re going to treat us like that. It’s a very clear snub to Ed Miliband.”

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Ed Miliband has been taxing and banning our oil and gas industry into oblivion. It is insanity which means we will lose the taxes, the jobs and energy security having our own oil and gas resources gives us.”

A government spokesman dismissed such criticisms: “We’re giving the sector and its investors the long-term certainty to plan, invest and support jobs with plans to replace the energy profits levy when it ends.”
data.author: The telegraph
